items.title: The Counterproductive Campaign Against Hasan Piker
items.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
items.content:
Welcome to the mailbag!

I was on vacation when the Hasan Piker discourse reached a fever pitch, and I have never been so glad to be off my phone. I briefly considered not writing about it at all, and then thought about doing something longer and broader about Piker and the ideological battles within the party. But there were a lot of questions about Piker in the mailbag, and then even more people asking me to answer them. So, the subscribers get what the subscribers want.

Before we get into it, a little housekeeping:These mailbags run every Saturday as a special feature for paid subscribers. Subscribe to get full access and drop your questions for future mailbags.

Don’t forget to leave your questions for next week’s mailbag in the comments section.

These mailbags run every Saturday as a special feature for paid subscribers. Subscribe to get full access and drop your questions for future mailbags.

Don’t forget to leave your questions for next week’s mailbag in the comments section.

Tony

What are your thoughts on the Hasan Piker should or shouldn’t argument on Democrats engaging with him.

Answer

Trump is currently engaged in a disastrous, illegal war that costs hundreds of billions of dollars, has led to global chaos, and spiked gas prices above $4 a gallon. And Democrats have decided to have a fierce internal debate about a Twitch streamer.

It really could not be more on brand and shows that we have not learned the lessons of 2024.

For those blessed enough to have missed this internal Democratic beef, Hasan Piker is a wildly popular progressive streamer and a vehement critic of Israel. The leaders of Third Way recently wrote an op-edin the Wall Street Journal arguing that Democrats should shun him:

Mr. Piker is anti-American, antiwomen, anti-Western and antisemitic. No Democrat should engage with him. All should seek to push him to the fringe, where he belongs … Handing Mr. Piker a microphone isn’t magnanimous or respectful of views that test your priors. Going on his show isn’t a sign of bravery, of willingness to talk to the other side, or to show up in challenging places. All these things do is support a bigot.

They go on to criticize Pod Save America because Piker appeared on a panel at Crooked Con. He has also appeared on Crooked Media podcasts on several occasions and will be interviewed by Jon Favreau on Sunday’s episode of Pod Save America.

While I think Hasan’s views are more complicated than his critics suggest, he has said a number of very offensive things, including several that lean into antisemitic tropes. Like many, I find those statements to be abhorrent. While he has walked back his statement that America deserved 9/11, he has stubbornly refused to apologize for the others.

I would also stipulate that people on both sides of this debate are dramatically inflating Piker’s influence within the party. He has an important audience, no doubt, but election outcomes don’t hinge on the resolution of this question — something Hasan would probably agree with.

That said, the idea that Democrats should not engage with him at all makes absolutely no sense to me.

Democrats are not platforming Piker. He already has a massive platform. He’s a media figure. If everyone stopped appearing on his show or having him on their shows, he wouldn’t disappear. His audience doesn’t go away, and his influence doesn’t dissipate.

Let’s take Third Way’s argument to its logical conclusion. Hasan Piker said horrible things, so no Democrat should go on his show. Joe Rogan has pushed dangerous anti-vaccine conspiracies. Should we not engage with him? What about Theo Von and Shawn Ryan, two podcasters with massive audiences who were pro-Trump but have turned against him? What about Fox News? Should Pete Buttigieg stop going on Fox and eating their lunch because of everything Fox has done?

Going on someone’s show is not an endorsement of their views.

Hasan has a massive audience — mostly young people who don’t regularly engage with politics and are generally skeptical of politicians and institutions. Democrats need to talk to people like that. Our unwillingness to do so is a big part of how we got into this mess. Back in 2020, Joe Rogan endorsed Bernie Sanders, and much of the party attacked Sanders for touting the endorsement because of Rogan’s genuinely offensive statements.

In hindsight, that looks pretty dumb. What Third Way is advocating for here is essentially the same approach: if someone has said something offensive, Democrats cannot engage with them.

That’s a nonsensical political strategy, particularly in a media environment where YouTubers, podcasters, TikTokers, and online influencers hold real sway with large swaths of younger voters.

Democrats should engage with Piker — and when they do, they should be willing to debate him. Hold him accountable for what he’s said and push back where they disagree. If you believe his previous statements were antisemitic, have that debate with him.

Some Democrats, like Abdul El-Sayed, who is running for Senate in Michigan, are going further than just appearing on Piker’s stream — they’re actively campaigning with him, as Piker periodically endorses candidates. (Note: some of my co-hosts are supporting El-Sayed, who used to host a podcast for Crooked Media. I am supporting Mallory McMorrow in that race and recently hosted an event for her.)

If you want to argue that’s a bridge too far or a political mistake, that’s a legitimate argument. I’m not sure I agree with it, but it is an argument.

My biggest problem with Third Way picking this fight is that it’s unnecessary, counterproductive, and demonstrates a fundamental ignorance of the modern media environment. A corporate-funded centrist organization closely associated with the Democratic establishment trying to cancel Piker is only going to increase his influence and audience. If Third Way is genuinely concerned about Piker’s sway within the party, this is the absolute worst way to address it.

There are legitimate discussions to be had about how Democrats can become more broadly appealing. We need to be competitive in more states if we want to build a sustainable governing majority, and too many voters see Democrats as extreme and out of touch.

But the problem with the centrist political strategy is that it’s all about taking down progressives, not building up moderates. Last year, Third Way waged a misguided campaign to stop Zohran Mamdani because they feared a Democratic Socialist mayor of New York City would be an albatross for Democrats in 2026. That also looks pretty dumb in hindsight.

The Piker debate is really a proxy for several other fights within the party — including how Democrats approach Israel in the wake of the genocide in Gaza and the Iran War. It would be better to have that fight directly than to make it about one online influencer.

The bottom line is simple: this is a dumb, counterproductive fight that distracts from the real work of defeating MAGA.

Ellen Bender

How much was last night‘s victory in Wisconsin about the work of the Wisconsin Democratic Party, and how much of it is a bellwether for the midterms?

Answer

For those who haven’t followed this closely: last week, Wisconsin elected a liberal State Supreme Court justice by a wide margin, and Alicia Halvensleben became the first Democratic mayor of Waukesha in 20 years.

I could not be a bigger fan of the Wisconsin Democratic Party. They are the best party organization in the country and it’s not particularly close. They deserve enormous credit for their year-round organizing and for training Democrats to take State Supreme Court races seriously. Democrats have now won four of them in a row and cemented a liberal majority for the foreseeable future.

That said, the political environment is the biggest factor in these wins. Grassroots organizing matters on the margins, but the margins are what decide close races — and Halvensleben only won by a few hundred votes. I doubt she wins without the work Wisconsin Democrats put in.

Andrew Simon

Thoughts on CA primary? Obviously some candidates need to drop out to coalesce support around the highest polling Dems, but it is hard when no one seems to be breaking out.

Answer

I wrote about this a few weeks ago. At the time I thought it was too early to panic about Democrats getting shut out of the fall election, but I was getting more worried as time went on.

And then Trump came to the rescue.

For reasons no one can quite explain, Trump randomly endorsed Steve Hilton, the Republican Fox News host. (I know that’s redundant.) My best guess is Trump was watching Fox, liked something Hilton said, and fired off a Truth Social post. Hilton didn’t ask for it, and Trump’s decision has frustrated California Republicans.

The reason this changes the dynamic: the only path for a Republican to win was for two GOP candidates to split the roughly 38-40% of the vote that goes to Republicans, roughly evenly. Trump’s endorsement will likely help Hilton consolidate that vote, meaning a Democrat will likely come in second and both will advance to the general.

Late-breaking Update

The Democratic field is likely to shift dramatically after the implosion of Eric Swalwell’s campaign over multiple allegations of sexual assault and misconduct. I would be surprised if Swalwell is still in the race by the end of the weekend.

Once he is out, his voters will likely move to one of the other frontrunners, ensuring that Democrats don’t get locked out of the top two.

Austin L.

My question for Dan is what are the primary factors potential ‘28 presidential candidates weigh when they are deciding to mount a campaign? Besides popularity after the DNC speech what made Obama decide to run?

Answer

Almost every member of Congress, Governor, and big-city mayor harbors some presidential ambition. If given a real opening, most would run.

It’s different for everyone, but the decision ultimately comes down to a simple calculation: can they mount a serious campaign? Can they raise the money? Can they build the organization? Is there a realistic path? Can they generate enough press coverage to get known?

And do they have a reason for running? Ted Kennedy’s 1980 campaign took a serious blow when he couldn’t answer the question: Why do you want to be president?

If you can’t answer that, you shouldn’t run.

Now, plenty of people ask all of these questions, get unsatisfactory answers, and run anyway. Some of that is delusion. Some of it is wanting the platform a presidential campaign provides — attention is the lifeblood of American politics, and running for president is one way to get a lot of it.

As for Obama, he ran because he could answer all of those practical questions, and because he believed this was a unique moment when voters were desperate for the kind of change he was offering. Even though running less than two years into his first Senate term was audacious, he felt that if he let the moment pass, it wouldn’t come around again.

History suggests he was right.

Sam W.

You and PSA frequently discuss the new media environment. Are you all aware that your pod’s dedicated subreddit (FriendsofthePod) has been taken over by mods banning users for directly repeating your own talking points about the Israeli-Iranian conflict if said talking points are unflattering to Israel? To what extent is it helpful for new media organizations to engage with their communities on things like this?

Answer

I'm learning about this for the first time, and I'll be honest — I have no idea how the moderators on that subreddit work. But I'll pass this along to people smarter than me on that front.

Okay, that’s it for this week. Leave your questions for next week in the comments section below.

items.title: The 25th Amendment Isn't Coming to Save Us
items.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
items.content:
Thank you for reading Message Box, a newsletter for people who want to defeat MAGA. If you would like to support this work, please consider becoming a paid subscriber.

Donald Trump has never seemed particularly stable. He’s always demonstrated a fleeting grasp on reality and is prone to incredibly erratic behavior. In general, you wouldn’t ask someone who acted like Trump to watch your laptop while you went to the bathroom at Starbucks, let alone run your country.

However, even by Trumpian standards, the President’s behavior in recent weeks has been incredibly alarming. It begins with the bizarre, capricious decision to wage a pointless war in the Middle East. Most people give more thought to their lunch order than Trump gave to bombing Iran. That irrational decision was followed by even more irrational behavior. One day, Trump says we are winning the war because the U.S. doesn’t need the Strait of Hormuz (we do), and the next day, Trump is threatening to bomb Iran back to the Stone Age because they won’t open the Strait. On Easter, Trump posted this unhinged rant:

And while that was bad, the post from yesterday morning was even worse.

Even if you put aside the intemperate language and the bizarre praise to Allah, here is the President of the United States promising to commit war crimes — in writing. I want to say that again: the President of the United States just threatened to kill more than 90 million Iranians. That is the textbook definition of genocide.

Trump’s unhinged behavior has caused many people to bring up the 25th Amendment, which allows for the removal of presidents who are physically or mentally incapable of performing their duties.

Dozens of members of Congress and several potential presidential candidates — like Illinois Governor J.B. Pritzker — have called for invoking the 25th Amendment to remove Trump from office. Democratic activists have latched onto the idea.

Should Trump be removed from office?

Absolutely.

Is the 25th Amendment a way to do that?

Not really. And Democrats should stop promoting the idea.

Here’s why.

How the 25th Amendment Works

Most people believe that a majority of the cabinet and the Vice President can simply get together and remove the President from office. It doesn’t exactly work that way.

The Vice President and a majority of the cabinet send a notice to Congress stating that the President is unable to perform his duties. At that point, the Vice President becomes Acting President.

The President then has the opportunity to dispute this. The Vice President and cabinet must reaffirm their declaration, and Congress must assemble and vote within 21 days. A two-thirds majority in both chambers is required to keep the Acting President in place.

Why the 25th Amendment Is Pure Fantasy

By now, everyone has come around to the idea that removing Trump via impeachment is an impossibility. If the Senate wouldn’t convict Trump on his way out of office — right after he sent a mob of his supporters to murder them — it’s hard to fathom a scenario where they’d vote to remove him now.

And the degree of difficulty on the 25th Amendment is much, much higher than traditional impeachment.

It starts with getting J.D. Vance and a majority of Trump’s cabinet to vote for his removal. Look at that roster of sycophants and incompetent lackeys and tell me which one of them would put loyalty to country over loyalty to Trump. I can’t think of a single one, let alone enough to constitute a majority.

Even if somehow that happened, you’d still need two-thirds of both houses to remove him. (For comparison, impeachment only requires a majority in the House and two-thirds of the Senate.)

And let’s say all of those impossible things somehow occur — we still end up with J.D. Vance as President and Mike Johnson as Vice President.

What a win!

Why Calling for the 25th Amendment Is a Problem

I really do get why people are calling for the 25th Amendment. We are in a dangerous situation and people desperately — and understandably — want a solution. These Democratic politicians also want to make the point that Trump is unfit for office, and there’s some value in that. Though honestly, I think most people can see it with their own eyes and don’t need Democrats to point it out.

But this public push for the 25th Amendment comes with real downsides.

First, it raises expectations for something that absolutely will not happen. I think it’s cheap — and a little disrespectful to our voters — to push an idea we know won’t lead to the desired outcome. It’s a gimmick for engagement and fundraising. We should be honest with our voters about what defeating MAGA is actually going to take, instead of chasing clicks and online donations.

Second, it puts the onus on the wrong people. J.D. Vance and Trump’s cabinet will face their judgment in the history books — and the afterlife — for the ways they’ve sold their souls to Trump. But they are not accountable to voters in November. We need voters to understand that Trump is only able to act like this because Republicans in Congress let him. They are the ones responsible, and they are the ones who should be held accountable in November. The best way to rein in Trump is not to appeal to his cabinet — it’s to elect a Democratic Congress.

I wish there were easy answers. There aren’t. Stopping Trump comes down to winning back political power. Everything else is a distraction.

The Message Box is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

items.title: Is A Blue Wave Coming to Wash Away Trump?
items.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
items.content:
In a world of non-stop bad news for America and the world, the political news for Democrats has been quite upbeat. Everywhere you look, there are positive signs for the upcoming midterms. Democrats are flipping seats in districts Trump won, and even when we don’t flip the seat, Democratic candidates are dramatically overperforming Kamala Harris’s 2024 numbers. Dems even flipped the Florida state house district that includes Mar-a-Lago. Turnout in Democratic primaries has been through the roof. In Texas, more Democrats voted in the primary than Republicans, even though Republicans outnumber Democrats in the state and there was a high-profile Senate primary on the GOP side. Trump’s poll numbers are putrid. His approval is now under 40%.

From a political — or frankly any — perspective, things couldn’t be going worse for Trump and the Republicans. This is the least popular Trump has ever been, and it’s the worst political environment he has faced in his career.

And yet, per Nate Silver, the generic ballot average is only 5.5 points — about 2 points less than it was in 2018 when the Blue Wave swept in 41 Democrats, and 3 points lower than the Democratic popular vote margin in that election.

This is why so many Democratic strategists remain slightly anxious about the election. A 5.5-point Democratic margin is enough to win the House, but it’s not enough for the Senate, and it’s just less than what it should be given Trump’s numbers, the war, and the state of the economy.

It’s fair to ask: Is the Blue Wave really coming this fall? Or are Democrats about to snatch defeat from the jaws of victory again?

Democratic Challenges

To do the most on-brand thing possible, I will start with the warning signs for Democrats. But don’t throw your phone into the nearest body of water yet. There are reasons for optimism, too.

The biggest impediment to a massive Blue Wave is the Democratic Party itself. Voters are abandoning Trump and the Republicans in droves, but they are not yet convinced that the Democratic Party is the answer.

The party has never been less popular, and this is a significant difference from 2018. Back then, according to CNN polling, the Democratic Party had a 44% favorable rating and was five points more popular than the Republicans.

Today, the situation looks much different. Democrats have a 28% favorable rating, and despite literally everything, Republicans are 4 points more popular than Democrats.

The party’s problems with voters also appear in recent issue polling conducted by NBC News.

Most alarmingly, 55% of voters think Trump’s policies are hurting the economy — but Democrats and Republicans are tied when asked which party would do a better job handling it. Similarly, voters have soured on Trump’s handling of immigration, but Republicans still hold a 22-point advantage on the issue.

Midterms are primarily referendums on the incumbent, which is why Democrats can be less popular than canker sores and still be favorites to win the House. But to truly maximize the historical opportunity before us, Democrats need to convince more voters that they are a worthy vessel for people’s discontent with Trump and the status quo.

Reasons for Optimism

All is not lost. There are real reasons to believe that 2026 could still be a historic opportunity for Democrats.

First, Trump’s political situation is likely to keep getting worse. There is no end in sight to the war, oil prices remain high, airports are in chaos, and the conflict has done real damage to the broader economy. Republicans are unlikely to be rescued by an improving economy in the coming months. To make matters worse, Republicans in Congress are about to try to pass $200 billion in funding for Trump’s war, which some in the party want to pay for by cutting healthcare.

Seriously.

Second, Democrats have a significant enthusiasm advantage heading into the election. As I wrote a few weeks ago, the NBC poll asked voters to rate their interest in the fall elections: 74% of Democrats say they have high interest, compared to 61% of Republicans. Similarly, a CNN poll found that while the party has a 5-point edge on the generic ballot, among those who say they’re deeply motivated to vote, that advantage expands to a massive 16 points.

Finally, Democrats’ repeated success in special elections is a positive indicator for November. As I cautioned repeatedly in 2024, special elections are low-turnout affairs where Democrats have an inherent advantage, and they didn’t tell us very much about the looming presidential race against Trump. I stand by those statements.

However, Amy Walter of the Cook Political Report has pointed out that in recent cycles, special election results have been closely correlated with midterm results:

For example, in special elections held during the 2018 cycle, Democratic candidates ran an average of 10.6 points better in their individual districts than Hillary Clinton did in 2016. In November, Democrats ended up winning the popular vote by close to nine points.

In the 2022 midterm cycle, Republican candidates outran Trump’s 2020 showing in special elections by an average of almost four points. In November, Republicans won the House popular vote by almost three points. In fact, in both midterm years, the special election overperformance average did just as well as the final generic ballot average in predicting the final House popular vote share.

According to data from The Downballot, Democrats have been overperforming Kamala Harris’s 2024 numbers by about 13 points in special elections so far in 2025 and 2026. If this trend continues, it suggests that the Democratic victory in November could be larger than the generic ballot currently indicates.

Where We Go From Here

There is a lot of time left until the midterms, and many things can happen. I suspect Trump is going to try to engineer a Supreme Court vacancy to gin up Republican turnout — though I’m skeptical it will work. It’s clear Democrats have some work to do. Even if party leadership is unable to improve the brand, Democratic candidates will need to run smart, disciplined races.

But at the end of the day, the polling — and my analysis — only tells you where things are right now. It cannot tell you where things will be in November. It’s our job to hop off the pollercoaster and do the work necessary for the outcome we want, and the country needs.

I know many of you are already volunteering, donating, and pestering your friends to do the same. If you’re still looking for more ways to help, I highly encourage you to visit Vote Save America for opportunities to defeat MAGA and elect Democrats.

items.title: Trump's Speech: He Declared Victory, But Admitted Defeat.
items.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
items.content:
Typically, presidents follow a decision to go to war with a nationally televised address to explain the decision, call for shared sacrifice, and define what victory looks like.

When Trump started the Iran War, he did not do that. Instead of addressing the widest possible audience, Trump posted a hastily edited video of himself speaking to an empty room while wearing a baseball cap — presumably because he couldn’t be bothered to comb his hair.

Despite 13 American soldiers losing their lives, hundreds more being wounded, global economic chaos, and growing opposition to his regime-change war of choice, Trump refused to take the basic step of explaining the war to the American people.

Until last night.

One month into the war, Trump finally gave a nationally televised address — but instead of offering clarity or rallying the country to the cause, he delivered an overlong, low-energy, rambling speech that served no strategic purpose.

Not that anyone should be surprised, but at the moment of greatest need for the country and greatest peril for his presidency, Trump failed to deliver.

He waved the white flag on his war and his presidency. It’s possible we will look back on last night as the moment we knew Trump’s presidency was functionally over. He’s still in office and can still do great damage, but the momentum is gone. All but his most committed supporters have tuned him out. It’s all over but the shouting.

1. The White Flag of Surrender

Because the speech was so poorly written, it’s hard to identify any strategic rationale for giving it. To the extent there was a purpose, it was to declare victory. Instead, it read as an admission of defeat.

This is a dumb and dangerous war, and I want it over as soon as humanly possible. American troops are at risk. Civilians in Iran and across the Middle East are dying. People at home and abroad are suffering economically. But it’s hard to read Trump’s comments last night as anything other than a concession that he lost.

This was a war of Trump’s choosing, and we can only judge its success or failure by the — admittedly disjointed — criteria he laid out. Despite what Trump claimed last night, the stated goals of the war were to replace the Iranian regime and destroy its nuclear program.

The Iranian regime is still in charge and more radical and anti-American than ever. The material Iran could use to build a nuclear weapon remains exactly where it was before the war began.

Yes, Iran’s military has been substantially degraded — but Iran is still a threat to the United States, and in the long run, Trump’s war has made us less secure.

2. The Crisis in the Strait Remains Unresolved

The biggest political problem for Trump is the closure of the Strait of Hormuz and the resulting run-up in oil prices. The strait was open and oil prices were low before Trump made the capricious decision to go to war. Now it’s closed and gas prices are above $4 a gallon.

Trump has no idea how to reopen the strait, so he plans to end the war with Iran still controlling the world’s most important gateway for oil.

During the speech, Trump argued this didn’t matter to the United States:

The United States imports almost no oil through the Hormuz Strait and won’t be taking any in the future. We don’t need it. We haven’t needed it, and we don’t need it.

With Trump, the line between dumb and dishonest is always blurry — but this is a genuinely nonsensical statement.

As Dominic Pappalardo, chief multi-asset strategist at Morningstar Wealth, explained to ABC News:

The price of oil is determined by global supply dynamics. So, whether the U.S. is an importer or an exporter, or regardless of what comes through the Strait that’s shut down, the price of oil is still set by global supply and demand.

Trump started this war and raised your gas prices. Ending it on these terms won’t lower them.

What Trump says doesn't matter here, because Americans can't be tricked into thinking gas prices aren't high. The fact that he thinks they can says a lot.

3. The World Has Tuned Out Trump

The most damning revelation of the speech is that the world has simply stopped listening.

The speech was intended to reassure the American people and markets that the war would soon be over. No one took Trump seriously. Oil prices surged and stock futures sank while he was still talking.

Presidential power flows from the ability to persuade. This was especially true of Trump in his second term — he was able to threaten institutions into bending to his will, someone to be feared, with markets and other stakeholders hanging on his every word. When people stop taking a president’s words seriously, that power evaporates and rarely returns.

4. Trump’s Grim Political Reality

After last night, the political picture for Trump is particularly grim. His approval rating is now below 40 in Nate Silver’s model

.

Trump’s numbers have been sliding for a year, but the decline has accelerated since the war with Iran began. A bounce-back is conceivable if the war ended and gas prices came down — but that would be a historical anomaly. Most second-term presidents never recover. They just amble off into the dustbin of history.

Trump’s path back to relevance looks especially precarious. The decision to go to war violated the two fundamental pillars of his political appeal — lower costs and no new wars. And as last night’s speech made clear, he can barely muster the energy to sell the war or save his presidency.

items.title: Is "F*ck Trump" a Good Message for Democrats?
items.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
items.content:
A couple of weeks ago, Illinois Lieutenant Governor Juliana Stratton won the Democratic nomination for Senate. Her seven-point victory on Election Night completed a dramatic come-from-behind win over Congressman Raja Krishnamoorthi, despite being massively outspent.

When I spoke to Stratton on Pod Save America a few days after her win, she said that the turning point for her campaign was this viral ad.

The ad was officially titled “They Said It,” but it quickly became known as the “Fuck Trump” ad.

Whether this ad was really the reason Stratton won is one of those unanswerable causation-versus-correlation questions. However, there is no doubt that the ad broke through and injected energy into a campaign that desperately needed it.

I find this ad fascinating, because the conversation around it brings to bear some of the biggest strategic questions that Democrats must wrestle with as we head into the midterms.

So, I ask the question — Is Fuck Trump a good message for Democrats?

1. Desperately Seeking Attention

Attention is the mother’s milk of politics in 2026. Every politician needs it and it’s never been harder to get. The old formula for putting your face in front of voters was two-fold. First, get the news media to cover you. This was never easy, but it was possible. You make “news” by announcing a new policy, launching a new effort, or saying something interesting in front of a TV camera. In state and local campaigns, that meant talking to the local press. Unfortunately, the local press is a shell of its former self. There are fewer outlets, and many of the ones that still exist do not have the resources to cover politics the same way.

The second way to get attention was to buy it. Campaigns would spend millions of dollars to run ads during the television programs most watched by their target viewers. While they still spend millions, those ads reach far fewer people in a world where streaming video is the norm and large platforms like Netflix don’t allow political ads.

Stratton ran this ad because she needed attention. She needed something that went viral online, generated conversation, and ensured that people knew who she was and considered voting for her.

Getting attention often means courting controversy — saying and doing edgy things that will get people talking. To get attention, you also need to be willing to piss some people off. The algorithms that distribute political news value engagement. An angry comment is worth as much as a positive one.

Running an ad with a bunch of Illinoisans saying “Fuck Trump” will get people paying attention. Many more people saw the ad on social media or through news coverage than when it ran as a commercial.

Getting attention is important. How you use that attention once you have it is even more important.

2. Why This Ad Worked for Stratton

When the ad was first posted, there was a lot of agitation among Stratton supporters and other Democrats that she had gone too far. People worried the ad seemed too desperate and would elicit backlash.

I was less worried.

“Fuck Trump” is a clever way to capture the rage that Democratic voters have — not just at Trump, but also at Democratic leadership, the media, corporations, and everyone else they believe has failed to respond to the threat he poses.

There was little worry about backlash within the Democratic electorate about being too anti-Trump, even with the profanity. Stratton was running in a Democratic primary in Illinois, a state Kamala Harris won by 11 points in 2024. There is no serious Republican running. So even if the ad was too much for some Independents and disenchanted Republicans, it wouldn’t hurt her in the general election.

The part of the ad that worked for me — and this is the lesson for other Democrats — is that once Stratton had your attention, she used it to tell the viewer about her biography, her policy stances, and that popular Democratic politicians like Governor J.B. Pritzker and Senator Tammy Duckworth were backing her candidacy.

Unlike so much of the viral slop churned out by various political actors these days, Stratton’s ad is attention-getting with a purpose.

3. What About the Profanity?

Just a few years ago, the idea of a politician running an ad with the F word in it would have seemed insane. Politicians never swore in public, and certainly not in ads and videos.

This is one of the many things that have changed since Trump came down the escalator 11 years ago. Our political culture has coarsened, and perhaps more importantly, politicians can now communicate with voters in ways other than broadcast television networks regulated by the FCC and their profanity standards.

Politicians are swearing all the time now. They call “bullshit” and add a “fucking” for emphasis to their tweets and other posts.

Look, I have no problem with profanity. If you listen to Pod Save America, you may think I have a profanity problem, because I swear more than I should (apologies to those who listen to the podcast in the car or at home with their kids around). There are limits — notably, Stratton and Pritzker don’t actually say “Fuck Trump” in the ad (although Duckworth does).

Even so, the bigger issue is that too many politicians see profanity as a proxy for authenticity. Adding the F word to your focus-grouped statement doesn’t change how voters see you. It just makes you look even more like a phony.

4. Can You Be Too Anti-Trump?

There are very few fresh ideas in political ad-making, so it’s likely that a lot of ad makers will see the success of the “Fuck Trump” ad and try to come up with their own off-brand version.

The biggest question raised is whether there is a danger in being too anti-Trump.

In the 2026 Democratic primaries and the 2028 presidential primaries, you can see Democrats being incentivized to follow the same path Stratton did — with or without a primary. Turn the contest into a question of which Democrat is the most anti-Trump. The good news for Democrats in 2026 is that there are no contested primaries in most of the critical Senate races in the red states we need to win a majority. While anti-Trump sentiment crosses party lines these days, there are limits to the efficacy of a “Fuck Trump” message when you need to persuade a significant number of Trump voters to win.

With a midterm electorate in this political environment — and Trump’s poll numbers where they are — I’m not really worried about Democrats being too anti-Trump. This will differ based on the partisanship of the district and state, but I just don’t foresee an anti-anti-Trump backlash.

In 2028, a huge number of Democrats will be competing for attention and grassroots donations. Being vehemently anti-Trump is a great way to acquire both of those precious commodities. Gavin Newsom has used this exact strategy to jump to the top of the hypothetical 2028 polls.

That may be a great way to win the primary, but it may not be the most compelling message in the general election. I don’t say this because there will be some fondness in the electorate for Trump, or nostalgia for the Trump era, as it comes to an end. It’s more that I think voters are going to be bored and tired of Trump. He will be old news. They are going to be desperate to turn the page on the Trump era and will be looking for someone who has a vision for what comes next.

In summary, Fuck Trump is a fine message. There is no need to pearl-clutch about it — but it won’t be sufficient to build the governing majority we need.

Please help spread the word by sharing this message on your democracy-destroying platform of choice

Her seven-point victory on Election Night completed a dramatic come-from-behind win over Congressman Raja Krishnamoorthi, despite being massively outspent. When I spoke to Stratton on Pod…

items.title: Janet Mills Is Attacking Graham Platner. Will It Work?
items.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
items.content:
Welcome to this week’s mailbag.

A quick reminder: these mailbags run every Saturday as a special feature for paid subscribers. Subscribe to get full access and drop your questions for future mailbags.

One note: No mailbag next Saturday, but if you have questions, I’ll try to get to them later in the week.

Bethany Reynolds

How effective is negative campaigning in party primaries?

The Maine Senate campaign took a hard turn this week, as Gov. Janet Mills released a couple of negative ads on her primary opponent, Graham Platner. I’m watching this play out in my social media feed, which is about 50% Maine Democrats. Most of them had already staked out a side, but there’s a lot of speculation about whether this is appropriate or effective, or if it will backfire. While I think a candidate’s past comments are fair game in a primary, it seems risky for a primary candidate to go negative, rather than perhaps leaving that to allied PAC’s. What does polling suggest about such tactics?

Answer

The Maine Senate primary has gotten nasty quickly. Even though the election isn’t until June, Janet Mills’ campaign launched a barrage of negative attack ads against Graham Platner.

Even for a high-profile race like this one, the timing and tone of these ads is notable. An Emerson pollshows Platner with a massive 27-point lead in the primary. Whether that poll is accurate or not, everyone seems to agree that Platner is ahead, and a slew of negative ads this early suggests the margin isn’t razor-thin.

It’s clear that Platner has the momentum, and Janet Mills is trying to stop it.

Will it work?

One of the sad truths of American politics is that negative campaigning tends to work. Voters are pretty cynical about politicians and prone to believe the worst about them absent other information. It works best in races where candidates are less well known, and the messaging tends to fill in the blanks. Oftentimes, campaigns come down to whether a candidate can define themselves before their opponent defines them. Kamala Harris entered the presidential race as a largely blank slate for many voters, and the Trump campaign defined her before she could define herself. That’s not the main reason she lost, but it’s a reason.

Negative ads work less well in primaries, though. Voters tend to be more favorable toward both candidates, and there’s greater potential for backlash against whoever is perceived as the more negative campaign.

Mills released another attack ad this week, drawing attention to Platner’s noxious online posts from several years ago.

In general, I’d prefer Democrats not spend millions attacking each other in primaries — but I’m not naive. Platner’s comments are fair game, and it’s better to air this stuff out now than in the general against Susan Collins. While people who closely follow politics already know about Platner’s comments and have either accepted his explanation or not, a lot of voters will be learning about them for the first time through these ads. Better to test the proposition of his candidacy now than against Collins in November.

Platner has a response ad up. It’s clear his campaign was waiting for this moment and likely had it in the can.

Mills’ ad is putatively about Platner’s comments, but it’s really about electability. Democrats want a candidate who can beat Susan Collins. Electability was supposed to be Mills’ calling card — the reason voters should swallow their concerns about her age.

The problem for Mills is that polls show Platner faring better against Collins. She needs these ads to change that dynamic to have a shot.

They very well could work. Maine Democratic primaries are dominated by older women voters, who are the clear targets of this campaign. The real questions are: do people believe Platner’s explanations, and do they accept his apology? If so, no ad campaign is going to matter in a primary.

Jerome Pandell

As an attorney, I didn’t enjoy Lis Smith’s “first let’s kill all the lawyers [metaphorically and as candidates]” advice in the New York Times but, isn’t the problem not lawyers running for office per se but candidates running statements by consultants and even campaign legal counsel before saying anything as opposed to speaking off the cuff?

Answer

Great question. For context: Democratic strategist Lis Smith said the following in an interview with the New York Times:

This is exhibit A of why we don’t need more lawyers in Congress and need people who bring different life experiences to Washington. Too many Democrats, when something like this happens, default to playing legalistic hall monitor and complaining about how Donald Trump didn’t fill out the right paperwork before launching strikes. That’s technically true and important, but that is not at all a persuasive argument.

I very much agree with Lis’s broader point that Democrats are defaulting to legalistic, process arguments in their opposition to the Iran War. One reason is that there are a lot of lawyers in Congress, and lawyers are trained to think in terms of legality and process. For a long time, a law degree was almost a prerequisite for running for office.

The other reason Democrats are defaulting to process arguments is that too many of them are worried about being painted as weak on national security or pro-Iran to offer full-throated opposition to the war itself.

I also agree with Lis that we need more people from diverse backgrounds running for office — veterans, small business owners, plumbers, farmers. But being a lawyer doesn’t make someone a bad messenger. Barack Obama was not only a lawyer but a law professor, and — while I’m certainly biased — I thought he was a pretty good messenger.

Sarah Robertson

Hey, Dan - question for Saturday’s mailbag. I read the following excerpt in a NPR morning newsletter:

“Republicans could use a budget tool called reconciliation to pass (a) deal without Democrats’ support. If Republicans go this route, they could use the opportunity to include a big priority for Trump in the same process: passing an elections overhaul package.”

Like… the SAVE act? The Republicans can slip in draconian voter suppression laws through reconciliation?!

I didn’t think that was legal (hah!).

Nothing surprises me at this point, but I’m extremely worried… thoughts?

Answer

You’re correct that, as originally designed, the SAVE Act should not be able to pass through budget reconciliation. For those fortunate enough not to remember the agonizing debates over what could be included in Build Back Better and the Inflation Reduction Act: budget reconciliation is a vehicle that isn’t subject to the filibuster. It includes the Byrd Rule (named after former West Virginia Senator Robert Byrd), which prohibits provisions that are “merely incidental” to the budget — meaning you can’t include non-budgetary policy changes dressed up as budget items. The Senate parliamentarian rules on whether each provision passes that test, and anything that fails gets stripped out. This process is called a Byrd Bath.

However, the Byrd Rule is a Senate rule, not a law. That means 50 Republicans plus the Vice President can overrule the Senate parliamentarian. They could also simply ignore her — something Republicans did earlier this year on a Congressional Review Act vote overturning California’s EV emissions waiver. That vote was different from budget reconciliation, but it’s a dangerous precedent.

To date, Republicans have resisted doing so on reconciliation specifically. And while 50 Republicans support the SAVE Act, it’s not clear that 50 Senate Republicans would support changing the rules to pass it. Republicans from John Thune on down have repeatedly said there aren’t enough votes to end the filibuster to pass the SAVE Act, and overruling the parliamentarian on reconciliation would be the de facto end of the filibuster.

So the guardrail is real — but it’s only as strong as Republican senators’ willingness to respect it. That’s worth watching (and worrying about).

Kris Hetz

Hi, Dan —

If Dems take the House, would they be able to “rescind” the SAVE act that’s now before the Senate, so it would no longer be possible to enact into law? I know the best course of events is to take both House and Senate so it would just die, but we all know the Senate is a longer shot than the House.

Answer

Unfortunately, no. They'd need to pass a law repealing the SAVE Act, get it through the Senate, and then get it to the President's desk — and they'd need a veto-proof two-thirds majority to do it. That's mathematically impossible given today's Republican Party, no matter how well 2026 goes.

Angela Pineda

GOP is looking to do an “unprecedented” midterm convention in Dallas at the American Airlines Center, per local FOX station. Does this show how scared they are to lose Texas?

Answer

I’m not sure whether Republicans are leaning toward Dallas for the “Midterm Convention” because of or in spite of the Texas Senate race — but either way, I think a high-profile, Trump-centric event in Texas would be, at worst, neutral for Talarico, and likely beneficial.

Trump is historically unpopular even in Texas. He’s incapable of delivering a persuasive economic message — his typical spiel about what a great economy this is and how much he’s lowered prices is a thumb in the eye of voters who are living the opposite reality. An event like this is almost certainly going to be intensely partisan and filled with vicious attacks, which gives Talarico a natural opportunity to contrast it with a message of unity.

Okay, that’s it for this week. I won’t be in your inbox next Saturday because of a family trip

items.title: The Republican Collapse Has Begun — And Trump Is Leading It
items.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
items.content:
Donald Trump’s stranglehold on American politics is coming to an end. The evidence of his political crisis is all around us. It started long before his ham-handed war with Iran and the resultant spike in gas prices, but recent events have catalyzed his downfall. Republicans are so far inside the right-wing news bubble that they don’t see the gravity of their own situation, and Democrats are so scarred by the 2024 election that we are struggling to process the changed political battlefield.

The most recent piece of evidence came on Tuesday night, when Democrat Emily Gregory won a special election for a state House district in Florida. Trump won that district by 11 points in 2024, and it just happens to be Trump’s own district, because it contains Mar-a-Lago. There is some incredible symbolism about a Democrat flipping the district that contains Mar-a-Lago. On the same night, a Democrat also flipped a Trump +7 state Senate seat near Tampa.

These results continue the trend of Democrats dramatically overperforming — and Republicans woefully underperforming — in special elections across the country.

Everywhere you look, there is bad political news for Trump and it seems to get worse by the day. Yes, it’s the war in Iran, the high gas prices, the long TSA lines — but it’s bigger than just the things happening right now.

It’s worth taking a step back to look at the political landscape, Trump’s standing, and where things might be going. This could be a generational opportunity for Democrats if we recognize it and take the necessary steps to seize it.

Let me explain:

Pitiful Polling

While much was made of Trump’s political strength when he took office more than a year ago, he started as one of the least popular newly elected presidents in history. Even still, Trump’s approval was above 50% in some polls for the first time in his life.

Well, that didn’t last long. Disapproval began to exceed job approval after he launched the tariffs last spring, but things have gotten steadily worse. Trump’s approval is under 40% in the Reuters, Quinnipiac, and AP polls released on Wednesday morning. Any individual poll can be an outlier, but three established polls all showing Trump under 40 on the same day can’t be good news for him.

As Laksha Jain wrote in The Argument:

Donald Trump isn’t just the most unpopular he’s ever been in this current presidential term. He’s more unpopular than any prior president has been at the equivalent point in time — including Trump in his first term.

The drop in Trump’s approval comes across the board. He’s down with voters of every stripe, but there is a particularly concerning drop among men. Overperformance among men has been key to Trump’s political success over the years, and he’s now underwater with men. It’s hard to overstate how politically devastating that is. It’s a mortal blow to the Trump coalition.

The correlation between presidential approval and midterm performance has weakened over the years. Democrats were able to hold onto the Senate in 2022 despite Biden’s middling approval. Republicans may do the same, but the odds get longer every day.

The Democratic Enthusiasm Advantage

The number of people who vote in the 2026 midterms will be dramatically smaller than the number who voted in 2024. While persuading swing voters is still an important part of the formula, success depends on turning out as much of your coalition as possible. Once again, a rabid, fired-up base has defined Trump’s Republican Party for a decade — but at this point, the GOP seems depressed, complacent, or both. You can see this in the large number of special elections Democrats have won in Trump districts in recent months, but it also shows up in the polls.

A recent NBC pollasked voters to rate their interest in the fall elections: 74% of Democrats say they have high interest, compared to 61% of Republicans. Similarly, a CNN poll found that:

Democratic registered voters are far more motivated right now than Republicans. While the party has a 5-point edge on the generic ballot, among those who say they’re deeply motivated to vote, that advantage expands to a massive 16 points.

Ultimately, the problem for Republicans is that Trump continues to drive intense emotions among his opposition, while his supporters are not as enthused as they once were. According to Nate Silver’s model, more than twice as many Americans strongly disapprove of Trump than strongly approve of him.

People who strongly approve or disapprove of the incumbent are more likely to turn out, so this is more bad news for Trump.

Iran War and the Cost of Living Crisis

Trump’s political problems are manifold, but his biggest issue is that the central premise of his electoral victory has collapsed. Trump won because people thought he would improve the economy and lower costs. He has done neither. In fact, he has now taken two high-profile actions — tariffs and the Iran War — that have raised people’s costs. Trump is now more than 20 points underwater on the economy and 30 points underwater on inflation. In the Reuters poll, Trump’s economic approval rating is lower than it has ever been — and lower than Biden’s at any point in his term.

If you had asked me a month ago whether those numbers could get worse, I would have been skeptical. Then Trump started a protracted, unpopular regime change war in the Middle East, which has spiked gas prices by more than a dollar a gallon.

The Iran War makes all of Trump’s problems worse. It hurts the economy. It raises prices. It has divided the Republican base — polls show about a fifth of Republicans disapprove of the war, and even more think it’s a bad idea or makes Americans less safe.

From a more practical, political perspective, the war prevents Trump from making an argument about the economy, touting his (limited) accomplishments, or trying to make Democrats an unacceptable alternative for disenchanted Republicans and independents.

A Historic Opportunity for Democrats

2026 is shaping up to be a historic opportunity for Democrats. While the Senate map, polarization, and gerrymandering put a cap on things, the fall could potentially look like the blue wave of 2018. States and districts that seemed out of reach just a month ago are now in play.

The Democratic Party has work to do. Party approval is still in the toilet. Voters still don’t trust us more than Republicans on key issues, and some of our strategies and tactics still seem to come from a bygone era.

While a few recent polls have shown a wide Democratic lead on the generic ballot, the average is still about five points — smaller than it was in 2018.

But the opportunity is there. We just have to take it.

items.title: Is Trump Trying to Steal the Midterms? (with Alex Wagner)
items.author: Dan Pfeiffer and Alex Wagner from The Message Box
items.content:
The shutdown of the Department of Homeland Security has snarled air travel nationwide. Republicans and Democrats on the Hill are negotiating a compromise that would fund DHS, but not ICE. Trump isn’t happy with the deal and is demanding Congress pass the SAVE Act, which would disenfranchise millions of Americans by requiring hard-to-obtain proof of citizenship just to register to vote.

Should Democrats agree to this deal? Will the SAVE Act pass? And if it does, who does it hurt more — Republicans or Democrats?

Alex Wagner and I dig into all of that and more in a lively Substack Live conversation.

Give it a watch — and if you find it valuable, consider becoming a paid subscriber. This newsletter only exists because of the incredible community that makes it possible.

items.title: The Surprising Politics of Long TSA Lines
items.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
items.content:
A month ago, the Department of Homeland Security shut down because Republicans refused to agree to any real reforms to ICE after two Americans were shot dead in the street. In any normal political environment, this would be a major, ongoing story. But the DHS shutdown quickly faded from the conversation because Trump started a war with Iran that is rapidly spiraling out of control and has triggered a global economic crisis.

The shutdown is back in the news this week, as videos began circulating of massive TSA lines at airports across the country. In some cases, the lines extended beyond the terminal onto the sidewalk, with passengers waiting more than an hour just to get through security.

The DHS shutdown is now disrupting travel across the country.

TSA was always going to be where the shutdown showed up in people’s lives. ICE and the rest of DHS’s immigration enforcement have plenty of money thanks to the so-called One Big Beautiful Bill, which funneled tens of billions into Trump’s mass deportation operation. TSA agents, though deemed essential and required to keep working, aren’t being paid — and many are understandably not showing up.

Trump upped the ante over the weekend, threatening to send ICE agents to airports to cover the understaffed TSA checkpoints. He apparently got the idea from a random caller on a Fox News show, which is probably not the policy pipeline you want for the person with the nuclear codes.

Most shutdowns end the moment air travel gets disrupted. Staffing shortages at air traffic control helped end both the 2019 and 2025 government shutdowns. The same dynamic may be playing out here.

This is normally the moment when Democrats start feeling the pressure and either cut an inferior deal or cave altogether.

They should not do that.

The politics are not what they seem. Democrats have a much stronger hand than it appears.

1. Voters Are Likely to Blame Republicans, Not Democrats

Most shutdowns are high-profile affairs that dominate political coverage, with intense focus on the stakes and the human cost.

Not this one.

It’s one department, much of which is already funded, and it’s unfolding in the middle of a war. Most Americans have been going about their daily lives largely unaware of the shutdown — until they get to the airport.

And even then, it’s not obvious to travelers that the lines are the result of a government shutdown.

And even if people in line figure out that a shutdown is the cause, I don’t think they’ll automatically blame Democrats. They’re much more likely to blame Republicans.

We saw this pattern in the last shutdown: all of the polling showed that a plurality of voters blamed Republicans, not Democrats, even though Democrats had initiated the shutdown and were very public about their reasons for doing so.

This reflexive tendency to blame Trump and Republicans stems from two things. First, Republicans control everything. When something isn’t working, voters hold the party in power responsible for fixing it. Second, Trump’s personal brand is such that whenever there’s a dispute or a crisis, most people’s default assumption is that he’s the one being unreasonable. The majority of voters presume he is at fault.

The key point for Democrats: as more people pay attention to the shutdown, the political pressure should fall on Republicans, not Democrats.

2. Democrats Have the Better Argument

The Democratic case is two-fold. First, they are demanding commonsense reforms to ICE following the killing of two American citizens and countless other examples of dangerous and illegal behavior by the agency.

That argument has real purchase. Americans are deeply unhappy with ICE and Trump’s broader approach to immigration enforcement. A Marist/NPR poll from February found that 60% of Americans disapprove of ICE, 65% believe ICE has gone too far, and 62% think ICE is making Americans less safe.

And immigration is no longer a political asset for Trump. According to Nate Silver’s model, Trump is nearly ten points underwater on the issue.

This is why Trump’s idea to send ICE agents to help TSA is a potential political disaster for Republicans.

The second part of the Democratic argument may be even stronger. Republicans are the ones blocking TSA funding. Democrats want to fund TSA and the rest of DHS now, while negotiations on ICE reform continue. Republicans have voted down bills to fund TSA alone at least half a dozen times.

Last night, Trump made the Republicans’ position even weaker by saying that he wouldn’t sign a bill funding DHS unless Congress passed the politically toxic SAVE Act.

That’s a powerful political and rhetorical position: Democrats want to pay TSA workers and fix a broken agency. Republicans are saying no.

3. What Democrats Should Do

Congress is about to leave for a two-week recess — during which many members will be flying through the same understaffed airports — so the shutdown may be approaching an endgame. Unlike last year’s shutdown, Republicans appear to want some kind of resolution. The White House has sent offers and counteroffers, which have been wholly insufficient, but the mere fact that they’re negotiating at all signals that Trump and his team feel politically exposed.

We also need to be realistic about what the minority party can actually achieve with limited leverage. Keeping DHS shut down indefinitely isn’t a good strategy. And there’s a very good chance Democrats control at least the House next year, which means dramatically more leverage in any future negotiation on this same issue.

With all of that in mind, Democrats should enter the final stretch of negotiations pushing hard for real reforms — warrants and a mask ban at minimum — and, if Republicans won’t move on those, demand they fund the rest of DHS right now.

That’s a fight Democrats can win.

items.title: Will MAGA Abandon Trump Over the Iran War?
items.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
items.content:
Welcome to this week's mailbag. There's a lot to get into, so let's jump right in.

A quick reminder: these mailbags run every Saturday as a special feature for paid subscribers. Subscribe to get full access and drop your questions for future mailbags.

Don’t forget to leave your questions for next week’s mailbag in the comments section.

Mark Davies

What will it take before we see Trump lose support for this war?

I am referring specifically about losing support from prominent R lawmakers and other higher ranking party types?

Answer

This may be the most important question in politics over the next several weeks and months. Trump ran as the candidate who wouldn’t drag us into wars in the Middle East — and then he dragged us into a war in the Middle East. He started a needless war with Iran that isn’t going well, has thrown the global economy into crisis, and sent gas prices through the roof.

Under the normal rules of politics, that should be game, set, match for Trump.

So it’s fair to ask: when does Trump’s own party walk away from him?

As usual, the answer isn’t as simple as it should be.

It’s worth noting that some of Trump’s most high-profile media allies have already broken with him over Iran — MAGA stalwarts like Megyn Kelly and Tucker Carlson, along with MAGA-adjacent podcasters like Joe Rogan and Shawn Ryan. Honestly, those defections are more politically significant than a handful of senators or House members going wobbly.

That said, the vast majority of elected Republicans have stuck with Trump, and most will continue to do so — even if he deploys ground troops, even if he writes a $200 billion blank check for the war.

Why? Because the base is sticking with him. Polling shows roughly 85–90% of MAGA voters approve of the war with Iran.

Which raises an obvious question: weren’t these the same people calling themselves non-interventionists five minutes ago?

They absolutely were. But I think one of the biggest analytical errors of this era is the assumption that Trump leads an ideological movement. He doesn’t. He’s built a cult of personality. His voters are for what he’s for, and when he changes his mind, they change theirs.

The deeper problem for Republicans is that the MAGA base is smaller than it used to be, and the new voters who pushed them over the top in 2024 are not happy. As CNN’s Aaron Blake noted, a recent YouGov/Economist poll found that 17% of Republicans — and nearly one in four 2024 Trump voters — disapprove of Trump on Iran. And even among those who approve of the war, the support is tepid: in most polls, nearly twice as many people strongly disapprove as strongly approve.

That’s a brutal position to be in — supporting an unpopular war that fires up the other side’s base more than your own.

In the long run, I’d rather make vulnerable Republicans own this war and let voters hold them accountable in November.

Joseph Jones

Let’s assume Iran is not resolved by November. What realistically could a Democratic controlled House (assuming Rs keep the Senate) do to move us to a path of deescalation and until end to the conflict? And when I say I mean realistically I don’t just mean what a constitutional lawyer would say the powers of congress are, I mean taking into account political blow back, not having 50 (much less 60) votes in the Senate, the Trump admin’s willingness to push laws and norms to the extreme, etc.? Would anything change if D’s had 51 senate votes? Is there any example in the past of congress stopping a president from fighting a war (or whatever this technically is) that he wanted to continue?

Answer

Democratic control of the House means Trump needs Democratic support to keep the war funded. Absent 218 signatures on a discharge petition, the Speaker controls what comes to the floor. Speaker Jeffries could simply refuse to bring war funding bills up for a vote. The Pentagon can shuffle money around for a while, but no funding ultimately means no war.

That’s an enormous amount of power — and a very compelling argument for working our tails off to win the House by a wide enough margin that we can absorb some inevitable defections when the vote to fund Trump’s war comes up.

Christopher Flask

Can I just say F*ck Fetterman? Maybe this is my question, what are the politics there for him with his vote to support Mullin? Is he trying to appeal to his purple base? Is it really going to serve him well given the unpopularity of ICE? His comment was “Why can’t we give Markwayne Mullin a chance?” Didn’t Mullin have a chance to be a good human when Alex Pretti was killed and failed? Isn’t there enough baggage on Mullin that Fetterman, as a Democrat, will take too much heat? Or does he feel it is too far to Fetterman’s next election that it doesn’t matter? I just don’t understand where he is coming from.

Answer

I got several questions about John Fetterman this week — many of which were significantly less diplomatic than yours — and I suspect that’s because I put out the call for questions right after he broke ranks to advance Markwayne Mullin’s nomination to lead DHS. Just the latest in a long string of moves that have infuriated the people who put him in office less than four years ago.

Honestly? I have no idea what he’s doing. He clearly went through serious physical and mental health challenges, and I don’t know whether that’s part of why he’s so fundamentally changed his approach to politics without any real explanation. What I am confident about is that this isn’t some brilliant political strategy — unless the strategy is to make himself a one-term senator.

Fetterman’s overall numbers in Pennsylvania aren’t terrible — 46/40 in a recent Quinnipiac poll — but they’re propped up almost entirely by Republicans, 73% of whom approve of him. Meanwhile, only 22% of Democrats do. That’s a serious structural problem, because Fetterman will almost certainly face a Democratic primary in 2028, and Pennsylvania has a closed primary where only registered Democrats can vote. Unless something changes dramatically, he’d lose that primary, and lose it badly.

He could switch parties, but that probably doesn’t end well either. For all his recent apostasies, Fetterman is still well to the left of even the most moderate Republicans. He’d face a primary challenge from the right, and even if Trump cleared the field for him, he’d be incredibly exposed running as a party-switcher in a swing state during a presidential year.

So no, there’s no master plan here. It increasingly looks like his goal is to irritate the people who elected him until they elect someone else.

Val

Hi Dan. My questions this week is: Why are Democratic representatives arguing the process problem of war with Iran instead of arguing on the substance?

They way the leadership speaks, you would think the biggest problem with attacking Iran is that they didn’t ask Democrats for the green light first. And with just enough Democrats voting against restricting Trump yet again, it’s hard not to see the case that it’s the party’s only problem with this war

Answer

Because they’re cowards.

They’re hiding behind procedural arguments to avoid going on record with a clear, full-throated case against the war. I wrote about this when the war first started:

There are two reasons Democrats behave this way. Many still carry a post-9/11 mentality, perpetually worried about being cast as weak or unpatriotic by Republicans and the right-wing media machine. That fear is an increasingly outdated notion in today’s political environment, but learned helplessness is hard to unlearn.

There are also Democrats who genuinely believe that regime change in Iran is a top national security priority and are willing to support military force, under the right circumstances, to achieve it. That’s not a view I share — I think it means failing to absorb every lesson of Iraq — but it’s a position someone can hold in good faith.

Even so: how can anyone seriously believe that Donald Trump, a corrupt, erratic wannabe dictator who couldn’t find Iran on a map, is the right person to manage that incredibly complex undertaking?

Fewer Democrats are hiding behind process arguments than at the start, but it's still too many. If you can't oppose this president launching this war, what exactly are you doing

Tyler Smith

Should Democrats donate money to candidates like Nancy Lacore in SC-1? She is a retired three-star admiral and mom of six and seems like she would be a star recruit in another district. With Nancy Mace running for governor, the seat is open this cycle. But SC-1 is R+6 and Cook Political Report rates it as Solid R. What should Democrats do?

Answer

I’m glad you asked this, because SC-1 captures some of the big strategic questions Democrats need to wrestle with as they try to expand the map. This is a solidly Republican district — Trump carried it by nearly 9 points in 2024. But Democratic overperformance in 2025 and in special elections this year suggests seats like this could be in play, and Trump+10 districts are exactly the kind of targets we’ll need if we want to build a durable majority.

It’s not a seat we need to win the majority. But it’s one I hope the party invests in.

Lew Blaustein

What are your thoughts about Focus For Democracy’s approach, which urges Dems and reality-based voters to donate to innovative vote-getting organizations rather than the campaigns directly, which you urge. They apply an incremental Dem vote generated/$ metric to the organizations they analyze and direct donations to those with the highest scores. The campaigns I guess don’t win in this competition. Why is FFD’s approach off base?

I’m not familiar enough with how they measure impact to say definitively whether it’s the right approach or not. But here are a couple of broader points.

First, groups like this are pushing people toward investments with longer-term impact beyond a single election. I’m sympathetic to that. Short-termism is a real disease in Democratic decision-making — we’re constantly chasing the most winnable races right now, which means huge parts of the country never get sustained attention.

That said, I’m also terrified of missing this specific window. If we don’t take the House, Trump gets two more years of essentially unchecked power to do enormous damage. If FFD has genuinely cracked the code on identifying the highest-impact organizations, more power to them. But absent that certainty, my general advice remains: if winning in November is your priority, the campaign is almost always a better bet than a super PAC or party committee.

Sara Danver (she/her)

Sara, a longtime subscriber, Friend of the Pod, and Vote Save America superstar, left this in last week's mailbag comments. It's too good not to share more widely. Also subscriber to her Substack:

A little more information on Dan’s excellent point about Vote Save America here:

1. Sign up for Project 218! This is VSA’s campaign to take back the House in the midterms this year, and they’ll give you all the info you need about the most strategic ways to use your time, money, and energy. That includes where to donate, and where to volunteer! Sign up here:

2. Here’s the link to the fund for flipping the House, but please note that this splits your donation between 7 different groups which means you *may* end up on 7 different lists. This is not the faut of VSA, it’s a function of Act Blue. I’m not saying don’t donate here, I’m just saying you might get more emails. I personally think Democracy is worth hitting unsubscribe for the time being, but you do you: https://secure.actblue.com/donate/vsahouse26?refcode=web_vsa_menu

3. I have a recurring donation set up to VSA’s Anxiety Relief fund which I *love.* This fund distributes the money for you to grassroots organizations invested in long term organizing, key VSA objectives, and other electoral priorities. You get a wonderful email each month detailing how your money is helping, and you *don’t* end up on a bunch of lists because they distribute the money for you, rather than Act Blue splitting up your donation: https://secure.actblue.com/donate/vsaanxietyrelief?refcode=web-funds-hero

Okay, that’s it for this week. Hope everyone has a great week. Leave your questions for next week in the comments below.

items.title: How AIPAC Lost Illinois (and Everywhere Else)
items.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
items.content:
Tuesday’s primaries in Illinois didn’t get much national attention. That makes some sense. Illinois is a solidly blue state. The Senate and four House primaries were de facto general elections. Whoever won the primary would win the general in November. Therefore, Illinois was less interesting than Texas, Michigan, or Maine, where Democrats are wrestling with which candidate is best equipped to flip a Republican seat.

To the extent the Illinois contests broke into the national political discourse, it was about the role of AIPAC, the pro-Israel advocacy group, and the politics of Israel and Gaza. AIPAC spent more than $20 million backing four House candidates and one Senate candidate to try to increase the number of Democrats in Congress who support the Netanyahu government.

AIPAC and Israel became major flashpoints in several of the races, especially in the 9th District, which has one of the largest Jewish populations of any district in the country.

Illinois became seen as a test of AIPAC’s strength and a preview of how Israel’s politics may play out in the 2028 Democratic primaries.

Despite two of their chosen candidates winning their races, this was a devastating loss for AIPAC. The results speak to the toxicity of their brand, the idiocy of their political strategy, and the dramatic shift in opinion on Israel driven by the genocide in Gaza.

A Loss is a Loss

AIPAC endorsed Raja Krishnamoorthi for Senate and Laura Fine, Melissa Bean, Donna Miller, and Melissa Conyears-Ervin for House seats. Only Bean and Miller won their races. AIPAC tried to spin these results as a success.

Patrick Dorton, the spokesperson for the AIPAC super PAC, told Politico:

We consider this a pro-Israel win. We are better off in the Chicago delegation than we were yesterday.

That’s pitiful, impossible-to-believe bullshit.

Let’s just look at the results.

The biggest contest was the Senate seat. They lost that one. In their two wins, it’s likely that their chosen candidates won despite — not because of — AIPAC’s help. Donna Miller defeated Jesse Jackson Jr., who held the seat before going to prison for corruption, and Melissa Bean is a former member of Congress who had the support of much of the establishment, including Nancy Pelosi.

The second biggest contest was the 9th District, which pitted Evanston Mayor Daniel Biss and progressive journalist Kat Abughazaleh against Laura Fine, AIPAC’s preferred candidate.

In that race, AIPAC’s candidate came in a distant third; a clear rebuke in the one race where AIPAC and Israel were the central issue.

A Failed Strategy

AIPAC’s strategy seems designed in a lab to hurt the very cause they claim to advocate. The consequences of their hardheaded, counterproductive approach are on full display in the 9th District.

AIPAC was hell-bent on defeating Daniel Biss — the popular local mayor who was the frontrunner in the race — even though his mother is Israeli and he has refused to call what’s happened in Gaza a genocide. Their beef with Biss was that he wanted to condition U.S. aid to Israel, a fairly mainstream position in this era. Instead, they wanted Fine, a candidate who would offer even more unyielding support. In the end, they pivoted to go after Abughazaleh with some pretty gross ads. But their preference for Fine was clear.

By going hard after Biss in a race they were almost destined to lose, AIPAC turned a periodic ally in Congress into an ardent opponent. He called out AIPAC by name in his victory speech Tuesday night.

The problem is that AIPAC demands complete fealty to their agenda and will spend millions to defeat candidates, like Biss, who have good-faith disagreements on policy. A similar thing happened earlier this year when AIPAC spent $2 million attacking Tom Malinowski in a New Jersey special election in an attempt to elect their preferred candidate. Instead, they inadvertently elected a progressive who is a far more ardent opponent than Malinowski ever was.

Their political strategy is abject malpractice.

It’s not just the candidates AIPAC backs — it’s the way in which they do it. AIPAC doesn’t run ads talking about Israel or the Middle East. They don’t even use their own name. AIPAC sets up innocuously named super PACs to attack Democrats on issues that have nothing to do with Israel. In Illinois, the AIPAC vehicles were named “Elect Chicago Women” and “Affordable Chicago Now.” In the 9th District, “Elect Chicago Women” attacked Abughazaleh from the left in order to depress her support. In New Jersey, they attacked Malinowski for voting for ICE funding in 2019.

It seems sketchy, because it is.

AIPAC has become so toxic that they can only run campaign ads anonymously and on issues that have nothing to do with their core mission.

The Long-Term Consequences

Public sentiment on Israel has shifted dramatically in recent years. According to an NBC poll, positive views of Israel have dropped 15 points since 2023, in the immediate aftermath of the October 7th terrorist attack.

This shift is primarily driven by Democrats, but it goes much further than that. Support among independents is down 19 points, and even among Republicans, it has dropped 9 points.

Now, of course, this shift is not because of AIPAC’s political strategy — it’s much, much bigger than that. But AIPAC isn’t trying to solve the problem either. They aren’t making the case for Israel or engaging with critics in good faith. They seem to believe they can bludgeon critics into silence with millions of dollars from shadowy super PACs.

The results in Illinois show that AiPAC’s strategy is doomed to failure. They lost the races that mattered and made themselves more toxic within the party most likely to hold the majority next year.

Please help spread the word by sharing this post far and wide. Every share helps.
