items.title: Donald Trump, Warmonger-in-Chief
items.author: First Draft | Zeteo
items.content:
On this day in 1948, President Harry Truman formally created the Marshall Plan, providing aid to European Cold War allies. In a move the current president might question, Truman kept his name off the plan, instead using bipartisan respect for Secretary of State George Marshall to smooth its passage through Congress.

Good morning, Mehdi here. Thank goodness it’s Friday? Normally, yes. Normally, you can relax a bit at the end of the week. But during this war, Fridays bring an ominous feeling to all of us here at Team Zeteo. Because the weekends, with markets closed, are when Trump likes to escalate. Will this be the weekend, the long weekend, that Trump decides to send ground forces into what could become a “bloodbath” for US troops? We’ll be watching.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ warmonger Trump picks the military-industrial complex over his own working-class base; women don’t seem to last long in the Trump administration; Tiger Woods calls Trump from the scene of his DUI car crash; and Hamas refuses to disarm until Israel withdraws its forces from Gaza.

Warfare Not Day Care, Says Donald

Do you remember when private citizen Donald Trump and then 2016 presidential candidate Donald Trump pretended to be anti-war? When he said US wars in the Middle East had “bled our country dry,” and railed against “wasting lives and money” overseas? When he repeatedly insisted that those billions should be used at home to “rebuild the USA”?

Today, President Donald Trump is spending billions of dollars every single day on his own illegal and catastrophic war of choice in the Middle East. One online tracker puts the direct cost of his Iran war at almost $40 billion in less than 40 days. The Trump administration is already seeking more than $200 billion in additional funding from Congress for the Department of Defense, to pay for the war and to restock munitions.

But that’s not all. Later today, the president of the United States will unveil his 2027 White House budget, which is expected to feature a massive and unprecedented escalation in defense spending across the board.

The expected 50% jump in Department of Defense spending, from $1 trillion to $1.5 trillion, would mean the US would be spending roughly three times as much on defense as all of the European members of NATO combined. It would be the biggest increase in US defense spending since the Korean War and would exceed the biggest year-on-year increases in Pentagon expenditures under Republican hawks like Ronald Reagan (around 20% in 1983) and George W Bush (around 15% in 2003).

But wait… there’s more.

Guess how Trump, our supposedly populist president, plans to pay for this ginormous hike in defense spending, which is predicted to add nearly $6 trillion to the national debt over the next decade once interest costs are included? By cutting federal spending on healthcare and social safety net programs for needy Americans.

You could not make it up.

Here he is speaking at the White House on Wednesday:

Yes, he actually said it. “The United States can’t take care of day care… We’re fighting wars… It’s not possible for us to take care of day care, Medicaid, Medicare, all of these individual things… We have to take care of one thing: military protection.”

If we had a half-decent Democratic Party, or a half-decent mainstream media, this clip would be all over every single one of your social feeds and be appearing nonstop in every ad break you see on television.

If we had an actual opposition in this country, they’d also be calling this president a war criminal. Because that’s what he is. And he is cutting public services here in the United States to pay for his widespread destruction of civilian infrastructure in Iran. After pledging to “bomb Iran back to the Stone Ages” in his White House address on Wednesday, Trump bombed a highway bridge near Tehran on Thursday, killing eight people according to Iranian state media, then bragging on Truth Social that there would be “much more to follow” if the Iranian government didn’t agree to a deal with the US.

The US military “hasn’t even started destroying what’s left in Iran. Bridges next, then Electric Power Plants!” Trump reiterated last night on his social media site.

This is the literal definition of terrorism: inflicting violence against civilian targets to achieve political ends. Israel and the US are engaged in undeniable acts of terrorism against the people of Iran and Lebanon. Don’t take my word for it. Ask two of Trump’s own high-profile supporters from 2024, podcasters Joe Rogan and Theo Von, who have both soured on Trump’s warmongering. This was their exchange on Thursday’s episode of ‘The Joe Rogan Experience’:

Got that? When Rogan said that “supposedly they’re trying to stop the terrorists,” Von responded: “That’s crazy though if you’re the fucking terrorists… If you wanna stop ‘em, fucking stand in front of the fucking mirror.”

Von seemed to be referring to the Israelis in Lebanon, but his words apply to the US in Iran, too. As Trump prepares to escalate his illegal war and expand his war budget, and as he is accused by more than 100 leading experts in international law of violating the UN Charter and committing “potential war crimes,” I ask again: Where are the Democrats? Where is the mainstream media? Why is it left to a podcaster and comedian who happily attended Trump’s inauguration to call out US/Israeli “fucking” terrorism in the Middle East?

🇮🇷 Iran War Updates

Death tolls:

Iran: Iran’s deputy health minister told Al Jazeera that at least 1,937 people had been killed in US-Israeli attacks. Iranian officials said earlier this month that nearly 20,000 people had been injured.

Lebanon: Israel has killed more than 1,345 people, including at least 125 children, in Lebanon, according to yesterday’s figures from the country’s Health Ministry.

US troops: At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.

Israel: At least 29 people, including 10 Israeli soldiers, have been killed and thousands injured in Iranian and Hezbollah attacks.

Elsewhere in the region: Dozens of others have been killed across the region, including four Palestinian women in the occupied West Bank.

And in more Iran war news…

Tehran’s warning: Iran targeted a water desalination plant and oil refinery in Kuwait and a gas plant in the UAE, Kuwaiti and Emirati officials said, as Tehran warned it would attack regional energy infrastructure and telecom companies with US shareholders if America attacked its bridges, energy plants, and other civilian infrastructure. Iran, without providing evidence, later blamed the attacks on the Kuwait desalination and oil plants on Israel.

Strait talks: A coalition of 40 countries, formed by the UK, discussed actions for reopening the Strait of Hormuz and released a statement calling on Iran to stop blocking the route. No countries signaled willingness to use military force.

Army change: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth pushed out the Army chief of staff, General Randy George. Outlets, including CBS, reported Hegseth wanted someone who would implement the Trump administration’s vision for the army.

Weaponry intact: Iran maintains about half its missile launchers and thousands of drones, CNN reported, contradicting remarks on Wednesday, in which Trump said Tehran’s missile and drone capabilities were “dramatically curtailed.”

New regulations: Iran and Oman are drafting a protocol for supervising the Strait of Hormuz after the war. Iranian Deputy Foreign Minister Kazem Gharibabadi said the plan was not intended to impose restrictions, but to “ensure safe passage.”

‘You’re Fired’ (Females)

Pam Bondi is out. Kristi Noem is long gone. Tulsi Gabbard or Lori Chavez-DeRemer could be next.

Donald “you’re fired” Trump is taking a scythe to his kakistocratic cabinet.

But, hmm, what do they all have in common? ‘Never Trumper’ pundit Bill Kristol seems to have put his finger on it.

🗞️ What You Need to Know

Parting words: Pam Bondi, who reportedly “begged” to keep her job as attorney general, said she would move to “an important private sector role I am thrilled about.” Reported reasons for firing included that Trump’s perceived enemies have shrugged off DOJ probes and the Epstein files continue to fester. Todd Blanche, once Trump’s lawyer, will be acting AG.

Shutdown punt: DHS remains partially closed after hardline House Republicans refused to pass a Senate funding plan Speaker Mike Johnson agreed to pursue. Reports said no fix will be possible until April 13, when the House returns in full.

Executive time: Trump said he would sign an order to pay all DHS employees. He did not say where he would find the money.

AI alarm: Maine is set to ban new data centers, the Wall Street Journal said. The aim is to buy time to study the effects on the environment and the electric grid.

🧠 Pop Quiz!

Kristi Noem, who Donald Trump fired as homeland security secretary, famously shot a dog named Cricket. What did Pam Bondi, fired as attorney general, do to a dog named Master Tank?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

🌏 Anywhere But America

🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza: Hamas said it was not willing to comply with Israel’s demand for disarmament as part of ceasefire talks and would only discuss the option after the so-called “Board of Peace” addresses key demands, including ending Israeli ceasefire violations and creating a plan for withdrawing Israeli forces from Gaza.

🇫🇷 France ‘intimidating’ Palestine supporters: France detained European Parliament member Rima Hassan on suspicion of “apology for terrorism” over a since-deleted Twitter post referencing Kozo Okamoto, who took part in an attack at Israel’s Ben Gurion airport in 1972 that killed 26 people. “The French police and justice system are being used to intimidate those who support the Palestinian people,” said Sophia Chikirou, a member of Hassan’s La France Insoumise (LFI) party.

🇧🇫 War crimes report: Human Rights Watch found Burkina Faso’s military responsible for war crimes, including the ethnic cleansing of the Fulani group, amid the killing of thousands and displacement of millions in the past few years.

🇲🇲 Myanmar cements military rule: Myanmar’s parliament chose Min Aung Hlaing, a general who helped oust Aung San Suu Kyi’s civilian government five years ago, as its new president – cementing the military’s power after the country held its first election since the coup, an election observers say was neither free nor fair.

🇷🇺🇨🇺 Second tanker: Russia’s energy minister said it plans to send another oil shipment to Cuba, as the island nation struggles under US pressure. The first tanker, allowed through by the Trump administration, arrived in Cuba this week.

💬 Quote Unquote

“You better just be breaking your back and working hard. Maybe get yet another job.”

That’s what New Jersey GOP Rep. Jeff Van Drew had to say to Americans struggling to get by. It’s almost like Republicans want to lose the midterms.

😵💫 WTF?!

“I was just talking with the president,” Tiger Woods can be seen and heard telling cops prior to his recent DUI arrest in this video released by TMZ. Yeah, he called Trump from the scene of his crash. Totally normal thing to do.

🧠 Trivia answer: She tried to take him from his owner — a 4-year-old boy.

📖 Weekend Reads

What the Birthright Case Is Really About

Trump went to the Supreme Court this week to hear oral arguments in his attempt to end birthright citizenship – a move Adam Serwer of The Atlantic says would create a “permanent, stateless, hereditary subclass” of some 6 million people by 2050. Serwer, who coined the phrase “the cruelty is the point” to describe Trump’s politics, walks readers through the history of birthright citizenship and how Trump is seeking to make “America white again.”

Israel’s death penalty law is not about executing Palestinians

This week, Israel passed a one-sided law making the death penalty the default sentence for Palestinians convicted of killing Israelis. Experts warn that the law will be weaponized to execute Palestinians however Israeli officials see fit. For Al Jazeera, Mariam Barghouti argues that the death penalty for Palestinians “did not begin with this law.”

Trump’s Secret Wars on the World Keep Expanding

Crunching the numbers, The Intercept found that in his time in office, Trump has dragged the US into at least 20 military interventions, armed conflicts, and wars. Like most things Trump says, his claim to be the “peace president” is just another lie.

ICYMI From Zeteo

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly and Akshay Gokul contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘manage subscription’ in Substack, scrolling down, and toggling the button for ‘First Draft’ off.

In a move the current president might question, Truman kept his name off the plan, instead using bipartisan respect for Secretary of State George Marshall to smooth its passage…

For Al Jazeera, Mariam Barghouti argues that the death penalty for Palestinians “did not begin with this law.”

Trump’s Secret Wars on the World Keep Expanding

Crunching the numbers, The Intercept found that in his time in office, Trump has dragged the US into at least 20 military interventions, armed conflicts, and wars. Like most things Trump says, his claim to be the “peace president” is just another lie.

ICYMI From Zeteo

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly and Akshay Gokul contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘manage subscription’ in Substack, scrolling down, and toggling the button for ‘First Draft’ off.

In a move the current president might question, Truman kept his name off the plan, instead using bipartisan respect for Secretary of State George Marshall to smooth its passage…

items.title:
Trump Wasted $3 Billion on Tomahawks to Attack Iran. Here's What Americans
 Could Have Got Instead.
items.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
items.content:
Donald Trump is costing American taxpayers billions of dollars for a lethal long-range missile to illegally bomb Iran, even as he slashes funding for public services at home.

The Tomahawk – a long-range, precision-strike cruise missile that can carry up to a 1,000-pound warhead – has already been used in at least one documented war crime. The New York Times reported that videos showed a Tomahawk had struck near the Minab girls’ school in the early hours of the US-Israeli war in Iran. The footage further proved that the US was responsible for the horrific attack, since it is the only country in this war with access to Tomahawk missiles.

One Tomahawk takes up to two years to make, and the latest model costs $3.6 million. The US has used over 850 of them in Iran, according to the Washington Post, burning through a limited supply of the costly missile and setting a grim record for the number of times the Tomahawk has been used in one US conflict alone. The same missiles were also used in Trump’s attacks last year on Iran, Yemen, and Nigeria.

The estimated 850 Tomahawk missiles used to bomb Iran so far in Trump’s latest war have cost an estimated $3.1 billion. Here’s what that money could fund instead:

School Lunches

The cost of the Tomahawks that Trump has deployed so far could have eliminated 15 years of school meal debt for kids at public schools. Children in the US who can’t afford school lunch accumulate $194 million per year in such debt, and around 18% of households with children are food insecure.

Instead, the Trump administration has slashed funding that would feed children in the name of fiscal responsibility. Last year, the Trump administration cut over $1 billion to programs that helped schools and food banks provide meals to low-income families.

Cancer Treatments

The average cost of cancer treatment in our country’s broken healthcare system is $110,000, according to the National Cancer Institute. The $3.1 billion price tag for Tomahawk missiles is the equivalent to a lifetime of cancer treatment for around 28,000 Americans.

Trump’s cuts to Affordable Care Act subsidies and his massive Medicaid cuts will jeopardize healthcare for millions, including cancer screenings and treatment. And a recent Quinnipiac poll published last week showed that Americans’ top financial worry is healthcare. The same survey showed that the majority of voters disagree with Trump’s assault on Iran – although his Republican base largely remains loyal to the president and his war of whims.

Food Stamps

The $3.1 billion price tag could fund one year of food stamps for 1.3 million Americans, according to federal data on SNAP spending.

The “Big Beautiful Bill” Trump signed into law last year will shrink SNAP funding by $186 billion over the course of a decade, and the 20% cut amounts to the largest reduction in the history of the anti-hunger program. Trump also used food stamps as a bargaining chip in his partisan fight over the previous government shutdown, as he attempted to cut off assistance to blue states and even defied a court order in doing so.

Nurses

The median nurse salary in the US is $93,600. Using that number, the US government could fund a decade of salaries for over 3,200 nurses, at a time when American hospitals face a critical staffing shortage. US taxpayer dollars have instead been used to bomb over 115,000 civilian buildings, according to the Iranian Red Crescent Society – including hundreds of medical facilities.

Solar Power

In 2024, the Biden administration announced a $7 billion initiative to provide solar power to low-income and disadvantaged households – which Trump has since moved to terminate.

According to the “Solar for All” program’s calculations, the cost of 850 Tomahawk missiles could fund solar power for 393,000 homes – an investment that would save Americans millions in reduced electric bills.

Instead, the US has invested billions in devastating Iran and its energy infrastructure, even as Trump claims he is helping the Iranian people by bombing their country.

Iranian civilians have paid the ultimate price for the US-Israeli war of choice, as health officials say attacks have killed 1,937 civilians in just the first month, and 24,800 more have been injured. The staggering toll includes victims from just 8 months old to 88 years. And a mid-March UN report estimated that the war had internally displaced up to 3.2 million Iranians who were forced to flee Tehran and other urban areas.

But as civilians bear the brunt of Trump’s illegal war, weapons manufacturers arming the US and Israel are reaping in profits.

Tomahawks are produced by Raytheon, a multinational arms maker headquartered in Massachusetts. In the first day of trading after the Iran war began, its parent corporation, RTX, saw a 4.7% spike in its stock, hitting an all-time high. (The company’s stock also jumped at the start of Israel’s genocide in Gaza and has steadily increased since then.)

And just weeks ahead of the war, Raytheon announced in February that it had entered into five long-term agreements with the Pentagon to boost production of Tomahawks and other weapons. Raytheon said many of the munitions “will grow 2 to 4 times their existing production rates.”

The Tomahawks are one part of the disastrous war’s cost. An early estimate by the Penn Wharton Budget Model calculated the total bill at $65 billion, and Trump is reportedly asking for an additional $200 billion just for this war.

items.title:
We've Spent Trillions on Our Pentagon Budget. So Why Are We Losing Against...
 Iran?
items.author: Zeteo
items.content:
Americans are used to hearing that they have the greatest military on Earth. An increasing number are aware they also have the most expensive military on Earth, especially after Donald Trump announced yesterday that his trillion-dollar Pentagon budget is the reason we can’t afford daycare, Medicare, or Medicaid. So, one month into the US-Israeli war against Iran, why is the US taking around 10 casualties perday (that’s without setting foot on Iranian soil), unprepared for sea mines, running out of air defenses, and seeing hard-to-replace strategic aircraft blown up on the tarmac?

The Egyptian military, which knows a thing or two about having planes blown up on the ground (Israel launched the 1967 “Six Day War” with a surprise attack on Egypt’s air force that is widely considered a historic strategic coup), might have the answers we’re looking for.

Like many Army officers of the so-called ‘Global War on Terror’ era who were expected to work alongside Middle Eastern partners, I had to study up on the Egyptian army. Required reading included a much-cited, embarrassingly orientalist paper entitled “Why Arabs Lose Wars,” whose author attributes the dismal record of Arab countries’ militaries to alleged cultural defects like penchants for “paranoia” and “deception,” and a “predilection to confuse facts with wishes” (sound familiar?), while underplaying the real reason modern Arab armies didn’t win many wars: That’s not what they were designed to do. Instead, they were optimized to protect authoritarian regimes from perceived domestic threats by quelling dissent and maintaining supporters with patronage in the form of contracts or jobs. Most Arab militaries have excelled at what they were designed to do, with the operationally unimpressive Egyptian Armed Forces emerging victorious from their skirmish with popular rule following the Arab Spring.

So, what is the US military actually designed to do, and how does that explain the failures of the Iran war?

The World’s Most Expensive Military Is Optimized to … Be Expensive

The Pentagon is the product of a distinctly American patronage system, where the top dog is not a monarch or strongman but a monopsony of weapons manufacturers who dole out campaign donations and in-district manufacturing jobs to members of Congress, including influential Iran warmonger Senator Lindsey Graham, promise lucrative second careers for Pentagon and State Department officials who oversee purchases, and further juice demand by funding major DC think tanks.

Congress and Pentagon officials “consistently prioritize the most expensive programs,” Julia Gledhill, a defense budget expert at the Stimson Center, tells me, adding that individual services like the Army and Navy welcome overpriced weapons since a higher budget denotes greater importance among the competing services.

This doesn’t mean US service members aren’t committed to defending their nation, nor that the US military can’t perform some missions effectively – just that when push comes to shove, profit trumps performance.

In fact, Gledhill says, “the arms industry is designed to impede readiness” on the battlefield, because systems that require more maintenance and earlier replacement – or, even better, a total redesign – generate more lucrative contracts.

One recent consequence of contractors cashing in on unneeded redesigns Gledhill points to is the troubled USS Ford aircraft carrier. Before a mysterious laundry room fire forced the Ford to withdraw from the Iran war, it made headlines for ship-wide toilet shutdowns due to an unnecessarily complex plumbing system (we can only speculate about the connection of both events to sailor morale).

The military-industrial complex may even jeopardize plans to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. Against the wishes of the US Navy, self-interested members of Congress expanded production of the dysfunctional, unproven Littoral Combat Ship, or LCS (this phenomenon is prevalent enough to be satirized in HBO’s ‘Veep’). Now, despite poor marks on training runs, the LCS is the Navy’s sole option for clearing any sea mines Iran lays.

The profit incentive underpinning defense policy also helps explain a lack of rudimentary protective measures that have already gotten some US soldiers killed. Underground bunkers are what leaders in Tel Aviv, Tehran, and yes, even the White House use when they want to protect themselves from modern weaponry, but none of the bases in Kuwait or Bahrain I served in ever had them. Compared to aircraft carriers and fighter jets, there’s not much of a lobby for digging holes.

Likewise, missile and drone interceptor shortages now plaguing US forces in the Middle East owe to Congress’s inconsistent interest in buying more, and especially to the Pentagon’s sluggishness in adopting the far-cheaper – and far more plentiful – anti-drone interceptors perfected by Ukrainian manufacturers.

Finally, the arms industry is the missing puzzle piece for understanding who the US is fighting alongside in this war. While the Israel lobby rightly draws blame for the counterproductive, way-too-close US-Israel relationship, do you think AIPAC and Benjamin Netanyahu himself would be so welcome in Congress if they declared that, instead of constant war, the survival of the Jewish state depended on pacifism, and so Israel and its Arab friends should stop stocking up on US-made weapons?

Big Spending and Big Bombs Are No Substitute for Strategy

Ultimately, blame lies with Trump, who started a disastrous war doomed from the start. While I don’t believe we are witnessing asymmetric warfare between the US and Iran, as some present-day orientalist analysis claims because a Muslim country is involved – all combatants are in fact organized militaries with advanced weaponry – there is an asymmetry of interests: The Iranian government is willing to sacrifice everything in self-defense, while Trump is willing to sacrifice very little in pursuit of glory and enrichment.

Trump is far from the first president to launch a war based on fantasies about US military power reshaping the world, but he is unique in his embodiment of the longstanding spirit of US defense policy: that war should make you rich.

Trump and co., cheered on by weapons industry-funded legislators, expected to launch a war to grab oil, shake down frightened Arab governments, and make a killing in the market. And they thought they could pull it off because even they themselves fell for the marketing material that promised their trillion-dollar Pentagon budget had purchased omnipotence, not fraudulently overpriced contracts and stock buybacks.

Instead, the most corrupt administration in US history was in a sense outfoxed by the masters of the trade, and I don’t mean Iran. He may not understand it, but Trump is learning the hard way what the US military is really designed for: Funneling taxpayer money to Lockheed and Raytheon.

Even with the wisest generals and a Pentagon free of institutionalized corruption, Trump’s war would be criminal, immoral, misaligned with any conception of US interests, and killing civilians by the thousand and terrorizing millions more. But without the war lobby that turned the Pentagon into a slush fund, we might have avoided this war in the first place.

Harrison Mann is a former US Army major and executive officer of the Defense Intelligence Agency’s Middle East/Africa Regional Center who resigned in protest of his office’s support for Israel’s war in Gaza under the Biden administration. He is currently with the group Win Without War and recently became a Zeteo contributor.

The views expressed in this article are the author’s own and do not necessarily reflect those of Zeteo.

Check out more of Harrison’s recent essays in Zeteo:

items.title: BREAKING: Trump Fires Pam Bondi… for Not Being Corrupt Enough
items.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
items.content:
Donald Trump fired Attorney General Pam Bondi on Thursday for, among other things, failing to put his enemies in jail.

Bondi is the second Cabinet member that Trump has ousted, after he fired former Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem last month.

In the days leading up to this latest sacking, sources with direct knowledge of the situation told Zeteo that President Trump had been privately trashing Bondi’s job performance behind her back for weeks, if not months. Complaints were multi-faceted, and included griping about her handling of the Jeffrey Epstein files debacle, which continues to haunt the White House to this day.

However, another recurring grievance of Trump’s, these sources recount, is that he believes she did a “very subpar” job (in the words of a person who speaks to Trump often) leading the Justice Department’s baseless criminal investigations into and prosecutions of his political enemies. In recent months, the president has repeatedly vented to confidants that the Bondi-era DOJ isn’t prosecuting his many foes swiftly or ferociously enough, and that the now-former AG was at risk of letting his political foes, in Trump’s phrasing, “get away with it.”

Sources in and close to the administration also add that Trump has at times fumed that Bondi and the Justice Department have not investigated the 2020 presidential election results as aggressively as he’d like; Trump of course lost that election to Joe Biden but for years has worked to turn his anti-democratic lies and conspiracy theories into Republican dogma.

In other words: one of the key reasons that President Trump is getting rid of Attorney General Bondi – who went all-in on Trump’s cult of personality, and has overseen a Department of Justice that has indeed corruptly gone after Trump’s vindictively selected targets – because she is not corrupt or authoritarian enough.

In typical Trump fashion, he announced Bondi’s ouster on Truth Social while calling her a “Great American Patriot and loyal friend.” He also claimed she will be taking a job in the private sector, and said her deputy, Todd Blanche, will step in as acting attorney general in the interim.

Several outlets have reported, and Zeteo can confirm, that Trump is considering EPA administrator Lee Zeldin for Bondi’s replacement, a staunch Trump ally who as a congressman voted against certifying the election results when Joe Biden won in 2020.

Bondi’s firing comes just 12 days ahead of her scheduled deposition over how the Justice Department has handled the release of its files relating to pedophile and former Trump friend Jeffrey Epstein. The House Oversight Committee voted to subpoena Bondi last month to compel her testimony on the matter.

A previous congressional hearing over the Epstein investigation in February devolved into a shouting match when Bondi – who faced the impossible task of defending the indefensible – resorted to insults and bizarre diversions. At one point, she pivoted to the stock market to avoid answering questions about her failures on the investigation and apparent coverup of her boss’s alleged ties to Epstein.

“The Dow is over 50,000 right now. The S&P [is] at almost 7,000 and the Nasdaq [is] smashing records. Americans’ 401k and retirement savings are booming right now,” Bondi said as Epstein survivors seated behind her looked on. “That’s what we should be talking about.”

Her performance on Capitol Hill was just one example of her servitude to Trump, which in the end, was still not enough to stay in his good graces.

At a Cabinet meeting last summer, Bondi lavished completely nonsensical praise on Trump – an enduring theme of her short, rocky tenure as the president’s yes-man in court.

“Mr. President, your first 100 days has far exceeded that of any other presidency in this country ever … ever,” she said. “Never seen anything like it. Thank you. Your directive to me was very simple: Make America safe. And, despite that, we’ve still been defending over 200 civil lawsuits filed against you.”

Bondi then read some numbers on Trump’s supposed fentanyl seizures, then said, “which saved – are you ready for this media?” She turned to the camera and said “258 million lives” – or about 75% of the nation.

And off-camera, she faced Trump’s scolding push – sometimes publicly – to weaponize the Justice Department to prosecute his perceived enemies.

In a September Truth Social post that read more like a berating text, Trump ordered Pam to hire his former personal lawyer, Lindsey Halligan, as US attorney, and to prosecute high-ranking Democrats that had dared to stand up to him, including New York Attorney General Letitita James, former FBI director James Comey, and Senator Adam Schiff.

Bondi’s Justice Department did attempt several prosecutions, including of James and Comey but they’ve all failed in court – and Halligan completely embarrassed herself.

No matter how upset Trump was with Bondi’s job performance, numerous Trump officials privately say that the president himself has made it harder for them to charge his political enemies, given how obvious and public he is about ordering up selective and vindictive prosecutions of people who pissed him off.

As AG, Bondi turned the Justice Department’s mission into serving the personal and political interests of the president. A recent ProPublica analysis found that the Justice Department had quietly abandoned over 23,000 criminal cases in just the first six months of Trump’s second term – including hundreds of investigations into terrorism, white-collar crime and drugs – all to shift resources to his anti-immigration efforts.

Bondi even explicitly instructed employees of the Justice Department to serve the interests of the president, even though the agency is meant to be independent.

“When Department of Justice attorneys, for example, refuse to advance good-faith arguments by declining to appear in court or sign briefs, it undermines the constitutional order and deprives the president of the benefit of his lawyers,” Bondi wrote in a memo dated Feb 5. 2025.

But Bondi’s fierce allegiance to Trump – even over her sworn obligation to the rule of law – still fell short for the Mad King.

items.title: Shouldn’t Democrats Be Able to Condemn Genocide?
items.author: Zeteo
items.content:
This shouldn’t be at all controversial. After two and a half years, Democratic candidates and elected officials should – and indeed, must – be willing and able to name and condemn the genocide that Israel has committed against Palestinians with US support.

It is not a question of whether Israel has committed genocide. Putting aside the long list of human rights experts and organizations that have come to this conclusion, we in the US have all watched the genocide unfold on our screens, and on our dime. Since Oct. 7, 2023, Israel has killed at least 72,289 people in Gaza – and potentially as many as 215,000 people – while forcibly displacing 1.9 million.

The genocide is precisely why Americans’ opinions on Israel and Gaza have radically and rapidly shifted – especially among Democratic voters. And Kamala Harris’s inability to separate herself from Joe Biden’s support for Israel’s genocide in Gaza likely played a significant role in why Democrats lost the 2024 election.

Unfortunately, we continue to see Democrats – from Senate candidates, to potential 2028 presidential hopefuls, to party-aligned think tanks that advise Dems what to say and think – refuse to call Israel’s genocide what it is. In doing so, they deny reality, and doom themselves both morally and politically.

If Democrats want to regain power and the trust of the American people – and restore America’s supposed standing in the world – they cannot assume they can dance their way around this issue.

‘Purity Tests’

The preferred rhetorical crutch these days appears to be that voters who want Democrats to condemn Israel’s genocide in Gaza are not attempting to gauge their politicians’ values, but instead are unfairly subjecting them to a “purity test” or “litmus test.”

Take the Michigan Senate race, for example.

In October, Democratic Senate candidate Mallory McMorrow agreed that Israel has committed genocide in Gaza – “based on the definition” of the word, though she did not use the word herself.

“I don’t care what you call it,” she said, adding that “our position should be that there is no individual life that is worth more than another individual life.”

McMorrow has since complained that the word “genocide” has become a “political purity test.” She told Jewish Insider on Tuesday: “People seem to be more focused on a word than a goal, which is why I have since said this does feel like we’re splitting hairs over the definition of a word and not talking about for most people, we want the same thing.”

Roxie Richner, a spokesperson for Abdul El-Sayed, one of McMorrow’s primary opponents, tells Zeteo: “Voters want politicians who will call things what they are. Abdul has been clear: The systematic murder of more than 75,000 Palestinians is a genocide. If you can’t even name that, it’s hard to imagine you fighting for anything at all.”

McMorrow isn’t the only prominent Democrat sidestepping this word. When Kentucky Governor Andy Beshear, a likely 2028 presidential hopeful, was asked last week whether Israel has committed a genocide in Gaza, he responded: “That’s becoming one of those new litmus tests that we said we would never do as a party again.”

Beshear said Israel has a right to defend itself, before arguing “it could have been done without a lot of the suffering.” Even more awkwardly, he said: “I put a lot of that blame also on Donald Trump.”

Of course, Israel’s genocide in Gaza began under President Joe Biden. His Democratic administration funded, armed, and defended Israel as the genocide unfolded. It continued under Trump – and Israel has continued to kill Palestinians with impunity despite the supposed “ceasefire” deal he negotiated. Still, to put a lot of the blame on Trump, while ignoring Biden, is simply wrong.

Banning Candidate Questionnaires

While some consider the question of whether Israel is committing genocide using US taxpayer dollars to be an unfair litmus test, others go further, framing the basic question as something you’d only reckon with on a college campus.

The centrist think tank Third Way, which aspires to be “the chief opponent of the left in the 2028 Democratic presidential primary,” recently published an op-ed asserting that the terms “genocide” and “apartheid” are “loaded words taught in social justice seminars,” used by “Jew-haters on the left.”

While the deeply unpopular US-Israeli war on Iran and vicious Israel settler violence in the occupied West Bank rage on, Third Way has set its sights on canceling leftist Twitch streamer Hasan Piker, an Israel critic, and labeling him an antisemite as part of its campaign to crush the left.

The group has repeatedly hammered El-Sayed, the Michigan Senate candidate, for his plan to campaign next week with Piker. So have his opponents, McMorrow and Haley Stevens.

Though Third Way has pushed Democrats to ban “candidate questionnaires” and “refuse to participate in forums that create ideological purity tests,” the organization on Tuesday sent a questionnaire to El-Sayed, while demanding he disavow a series of statements from Piker.

On Wednesday, Politico reported on Third Way’s questionnaire for El-Sayed, cheekily describing it as a “Purity Test.” Third Way co-founder Matt Bennett responded with alarm, asking: “is it really a ‘purity test’ to ask a Democratic candidate if he agrees with the bigoted views of someone he plans to campaign with?”

Zeteo reached out to spokespeople for McMorrow, Beshear, and Third Way to ask if they are willing to characterize Israel’s genocide in Gaza as a genocide.

“Yeah, nothing says it’s a straight and not at all loaded question like asking if we will ‘characterize a genocide as a genocide,’” Bennett from Third Way responded. “Hard pass.”

McMorrow and Beshear’s teams did not reply.

Toxic Politics

To be clear, there are issues that Democratic voters, by and large, consider to be a litmus test as they choose candidates – abortion being the most obvious one. There are exceedingly few federal Democratic candidates who oppose abortion rights – and there’s nothing wrong with this.

If Democratic voters want politicians to restore the federal right to an abortion, they need to elect candidates who view this as necessary.

Likewise, if Democratic voters want to see their politicians stop arming Israel, and don’t wish to see their party support and fund genocide, they need to elect candidates who oppose Israel’s genocide.

That Israel has committed genocide has become an increasingly mainstream view in the US, and particularly among Democrats.

A poll conducted for Zeteo last year found that US voters believe Israel is committing genocide by a margin of 47% to 33%. Weeks later, a Quinnipiac poll similarly found US voters agree that Israel is committing genocide by a margin of 50% to 35%. This included 77% of Democrats.

A poll conducted in October found that American Jews resoundingly believe Israel has committed war crimes in Gaza, 61% to 29%. About four in 10 American Jews said Israel is guilty of genocide.

Recent polling from Gallup found that Americans, by a margin of 41% to 36%, say they sympathize more with Palestinians than Israelis. And nearly two-thirds of Democrats feel this way, compared to 17% who sympathize more with Israelis.

Even if Democratic politicians are not concerned with the moral stakes, and only the politics, they should view the question of genocide with more urgency.

On Sunday, Democratic Rep. Pramila Jayapal, the chair emerita of the Congressional Progressive Caucus, posted a video on Twitter, speaking to the consequences of US and Democratic support for Israel’s violence.

“Democrats are finally admitting it. Harris lost support over Gaza. And here’s the thing: I was warning Biden and Harris for over a year, personally, in numerous direct conversations with each of them. I kept saying that our refusal to hold the Israeli government accountable over the destruction and genocide in Gaza was morally, legally, and politically wrong,” Jayapal said.

She continued: “We kept being told, ‘it’s not in the numbers,’ that changing course was too risky or too liberal. But that’s not strategy. It’s risk aversion dressed up as wisdom. And it didn’t just shape an election, it helped shape a crisis.”

items.title: Has Iran Broken Trump?
items.author: First Draft | Zeteo
items.content:
On this day in 1992, a New York jury found mob boss John Gotti guilty on 13 counts, including murder. A key witness was Sammy “The Bull” Gravano, a former Gotti lieutenant who confessed to 19 killings and who would one day make headlines by endorsing Donald Trump. “We need a gangster,” Gravano said in 2024.

Good morning! Swin and Andrew here. Moments before President Donald Trump went live last night, Swin predicted that his Wednesday night address on the Iran war would sound eerily similar to that scene from ‘A Fish Called Wanda’ in which the Kevin Kline character, gun drawn, angrily insists that America did not lose the Vietnam War. Well, Swin was wrong… Trump’s so-called speech was much, much worse than that.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we review that sleepy, bumbling, bloodthirsty primetime address, the wider Iran war news produces more nasty polling for the president, and even Trump’s pet Supreme Court justices seem skeptical about his bid to end birthright citizenship.

Like ‘Listening to Joe Biden’

As we reported in yesterday morning’s‘First Draft,’ very few people – including in Trump’s own administration – are actually buying his effort to declare victory in Iran and move on. “However poorly you think the war is going, it is worse,” one senior administration official said in recent days. A different senior US official bluntly stated: “Iran can declare victory, too,” adding that “nobody will buy our attempt to sell this as a big win.”

Still, if you are the US commander in chief, and you’re one month into a major war that you launched, the one communications job you have is to be able to go on live TV and project calm, confidence, and reasonably high energy to the American people, when you’re telling them how well the war is going.

On Wednesday night – April Fools’ Day, funnily enough – President Trump couldn’t even be bothered to do that. (He’s a former reality TV star; he is supposed to be good at doing TV.) Setting aside for a moment the typically incoherent jumble that pervaded his televised address, the American president delivered a jarringly listless, elderly-seeming speech that did little to inspire confidence – including in his own ranks.

During and after his address, an array of Trump advisers, administration officials, allies on Capitol Hill, and rich Mar-a-Lago buddies gave Zeteo their snap reviews of Trump’s message and delivery. (Yes, they asked for the cloak of anonymity, so as to not piss off God King Donald.) Virtually across the board, the president was panned by his own people, with some denigrating the speech as pointless, and others reiterating how much senior members of the administration never wanted this to happen in the first place.

One Trump administration official said the following on Wednesday night: “It reminded me of listening to Joe Biden speak.”

In Trumplandia, that is perhaps the worst possible thing you could say about anyone, much less the sitting president and leader of the GOP.

But Wednesday night’s speech revealed the extent to which Trump’s reckless and illegal war is breaking him down – in ways that numerous other massive crises, criminal investigations, and scandals simply could not. For once, his bullshitting, bullying, and bravado aren’t working – and he cannot take it. Plus, he’s sounding notably old and drained as he tries to pretend, to a late-night audience of millions, that he can.

Before the speech, Politico reported that Trump intended to use his address to tell Americans the war is winding down. But the visibly deteriorating president didn’t seem prepared to pick a lane, and viewers were hard-pressed to find a message that didn’t sound like it was scrawled in a notebook by a serial killer.

Trump said his “core strategic objectives are nearing completion” – but the war isn’t over. “We are going to finish the job,” he said. “And we’re going to finish it very fast. We’re getting very close.”

Trump said that Iran’s Air Force and Navy are “gone.” He claimed that Iran’s “missiles are just about used up,” even though Iran on Wednesday launched its biggest missile attack on Israel in weeks.

Speaking of Iran’s attacks on oil tankers in the Strait of Hormuz – which have closed the world’s most important waterway, cut off the flow of oil, and spiked energy prices around the globe – Trump suggested that other countries should go deal with it. “Go to the strait and just take it, protect it, use it for yourself,” he said. “Iran has been essentially decimated. The hard part is done, so it should be easy.” He added that “when this conflict is over, the strait will open up naturally.”

After claiming, again, that his objectives were almost complete, Trump said: “We’re going to hit them extremely hard over the next two to three weeks, we’re going to bring them back to the stone ages.” (This is a threat of war crimes, to be clear.)

“Regime change was not our goal,” Trump added – a complete lie – before adding that the new leaders in Iran negotiating with the US are “less radical and much more reasonable.”

He quickly threatened that, if he and Iran can’t reach a deal, the US will carry out even more war crimes: “If there’s no deal, we are going to hit each and every one of their electric generating plants very hard and probably simultaneously.” He said the US could “hit their oil,” too.

“The whole world is watching,” Trump said, adding that “they can’t believe what they’re seeing – the brilliance of the United States military.”

When it came to paying tribute to members of the military who have died in his war, Trump muffed his lines. “As we celebrate this progress,” he said, “we think especially of the 13 American warriors who have laid down their lives in this fight to prevent our children from ever having to face a nuclear Iran. Twice this past month, I’ve traveled to Dover Air Force Base, and it’s been something.”

It’s been “something,” alright.

“We are on the cusp of ending Iran’s sinister threat to America and the world,” Trump concluded.

There is indeed a blundering, omnipresent, and sinister threat to the globe. Sadly for Trump, an increasing percentage of the world community and our own allies now identify that threat as a wholly American one.

🇮🇷 Iran War Updates

Death tolls:

Iran: Iran’s deputy health minister told Al Jazeera that at least 1,937 people had been killed in US-Israeli attacks. Iranian officials said earlier this month that nearly 20,000 people had been injured.

Lebanon: Israel has killed more than 1,315 people, including at least 125 children, in Lebanon, according to yesterday’s figures from the country’s Health Ministry.

US troops: At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.

Israel: At least 29 people, including 10 Israeli soldiers, have been killed and thousands injured in Iranian and Hezbollah attacks.

Elsewhere in the region: Dozens of others have been killed across the region, including four Palestinian women in the occupied West Bank.

And in more Iran war news…

Boots on the ground: US officials are planning two operations in Iran, one to capture Kharg Island and one to seize Tehran’s uranium, and await only Donald Trump’s order to proceed, The Atlantic reported, citing three sources.

Claim denied: A senior Iranian official denied Trump’s claim that the “new regime president” in Tehran had asked for a ceasefire.

Open letter: Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian said his people “harbor no enmity toward other nations, including the people of America.”

Diplomat hit: Dr. Kamal Kharazi, a former Iranian foreign minister, was reportedly injured in an airstrike that killed his wife. Kharazi was said to have been overseeing engagement with Pakistan as a possible host for peace talks.

Willing partner: According to the Wall Street Journal, the United Arab Emirates wants to join the US and Israel in using force to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. No Arab nation has yet committed to a military response to attacks from Iran.

Poll plunge: Just 34% of Americans at least somewhat approve of the war in Iran, a CNN poll said, a seven-point drop since airstrikes began. The survey showed strong opposition up 12 points, to 43%.

🗞️ What You Need to Know

Supreme skeptics: The Supreme Court seemed set to rule against Trump’s executive order restricting birthright citizenship, after his administration presented an extremely weak argument and he briefly attended the session, apparently seeking to intimidate the justices.

Pam in a jam: As he grows frustrated with the DOJ’s lack of successful action against his political foes and its handling of the Epstein files, Trump has discussed replacing Attorney General Pam Bondi with EPA chief Lee Zeldin, the New York Times reported.

House folds: Deciding to eat what he recently called a “crap sandwich,” Speaker Mike Johnson said Republicans would approve the Senate DHS funding proposal despite resistance from right-wingers and pass ICE/CBP funding through reconciliation, hopefully ending the longest partial shutdown in history.

Tone shift: Rahm Emmanuel, a potential Democratic presidential candidate, said he did not support Israel receiving weapons funded by US taxpayers. He spoke after a potential rival, Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, came out against all military aid to Israel.

Ron rampant: Florida Governor Ron DeSantis signed a voter suppression law similar to Trump’s federal SAVE Act, requiring proof of citizenship for voter registration and providing for efforts to un-enroll voters who do not comply.

🧠 Pop Quiz!

Donald Trump is said to be frustrated with Attorney General Pam Bondi and may replace her with Lee Zeldin. As reported by ProPublica, what charge against Trump’s enemies, probed by Bondi’s DOJ without gaining convictions, might be leveled at Zeldin too?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

🌏 Anywhere But America

🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza: Israeli forces opened fire in multiple areas of Gaza, injuring three people, including a child. In east Khan Younis, Israel conducted large-scale blasting operations on civilian homes, the Palestinian Wafa news agency reported.

🇧🇮 Deadly explosion: At least 13 people were killed, and 57 were injured in an explosion at a military base in Burundi’s economic capital, Bujumbura. An army spokesperson said a massive fire destroyed houses, buildings, vehicles, and military equipment.

🇦🇫🇵🇰 Ceasefire talks: Afghanistan and Pakistan are reportedly open to negotiating a peace deal in China to end their years-long conflict, per Reuters. Officials from both countries have traveled to the city of Urumqi to start talks.

🇭🇹 UN intervenes: A Gang Suppression Force official arrived in Haiti alongside troops from Chad to aid Haitian security forces in combating gang violence.

🙄 WTF?!

… at the White House, in Holy Week, Pastor Paula White compared Trump to Jesus: “Mr. President, no one has paid the price like you have paid the price. It almost cost you your life. You were betrayed and arrested and falsely accused. It’s a familiar pattern that our Lord and Savior showed us but it didn’t end there for him, and it didn’t end there for you.” Dear God, as it were…

🧠 Trivia answer: Mortgage fraud (which Zeldin denies).

ICYMI From Zeteo

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly and Akshay Gokul contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘manage subscription’ in Substack, scrolling down, and toggling the button for ‘First Draft’ off.

items.title: Has Iran Broken Trump?
items.author: First Draft | Zeteo
items.content:
On this day in 1992, a New York jury found mob boss John Gotti guilty on 13 counts, including murder. A key witness was Sammy “The Bull” Gravano, a former Gotti lieutenant who confessed to 19 killings and who would one day make headlines by endorsing Donald Trump. “We need a gangster,” Gravano said in 2024.

Good morning! Swin and Andrew here. Moments before President Donald Trump went live last night, Swin predicted that his Wednesday night address on the Iran war would sound eerily similar to that scene from ‘A Fish Called Wanda’ in which the Kevin Kline character, gun drawn, angrily insists that America did not lose the Vietnam War. Well, Swin was wrong… Trump’s so-called speech was much, much worse than that.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we review that sleepy, bumbling, bloodthirsty primetime address, the wider Iran war news produces more nasty polling for the president, and even Trump’s pet Supreme Court justices seem skeptical about his bid to end birthright citizenship.

Like ‘Listening to Joe Biden’

As we reported in yesterday morning’s‘First Draft,’ very few people – including in Trump’s own administration – are actually buying his effort to declare victory in Iran and move on. “However poorly you think the war is going, it is worse,” one senior administration official said in recent days. A different senior US official bluntly stated: “Iran can declare victory, too,” adding that “nobody will buy our attempt to sell this as a big win.”

Still, if you are the US commander in chief, and you’re one month into a major war that you launched, the one communications job you have is to be able to go on live TV and project calm, confidence, and reasonably high energy to the American people, when you’re telling them how well the war is going.

On Wednesday night – April Fools’ Day, funnily enough – President Trump couldn’t even be bothered to do that. (He’s a former reality TV star; he is supposed to be good at doing TV.) Setting aside for a moment the typically incoherent jumble that pervaded his televised address, the American president delivered a jarringly listless, elderly-seeming speech that did little to inspire confidence – including in his own ranks.

During and after his address, an array of Trump advisers, administration officials, allies on Capitol Hill, and rich Mar-a-Lago buddies gave Zeteo their snap reviews of Trump’s message and delivery. (Yes, they asked for the cloak of anonymity, so as to not piss off God King Donald.) Virtually across the board, the president was panned by his own people, with some denigrating the speech as pointless, and others reiterating how much senior members of the administration never wanted this to happen in the first place.

One Trump administration official said the following on Wednesday night: “It reminded me of listening to Joe Biden speak.”

In Trumplandia, that is perhaps the worst possible thing you could say about anyone, much less the sitting president and leader of the GOP.

But Wednesday night’s speech revealed the extent to which Trump’s reckless and illegal war is breaking him down – in ways that numerous other massive crises, criminal investigations, and scandals simply could not. For once, his bullshitting, bullying, and bravado aren’t working – and he cannot take it. Plus, he’s sounding notably old and drained as he tries to pretend, to a late-night audience of millions, that he can.

Before the speech, Politico reported that Trump intended to use his address to tell Americans the war is winding down. But the visibly deteriorating president didn’t seem prepared to pick a lane, and viewers were hard-pressed to find a message that didn’t sound like it was scrawled in a notebook by a serial killer.

Trump said his “core strategic objectives are nearing completion” – but the war isn’t over. “We are going to finish the job,” he said. “And we’re going to finish it very fast. We’re getting very close.”

Trump said that Iran’s Air Force and Navy are “gone.” He claimed that Iran’s “missiles are just about used up,” even though Iran on Wednesday launched its biggest missile attack on Israel in weeks.

Speaking of Iran’s attacks on oil tankers in the Strait of Hormuz – which have closed the world’s most important waterway, cut off the flow of oil, and spiked energy prices around the globe – Trump suggested that other countries should go deal with it. “Go to the strait and just take it, protect it, use it for yourself,” he said. “Iran has been essentially decimated. The hard part is done, so it should be easy.” He added that “when this conflict is over, the strait will open up naturally.”

After claiming, again, that his objectives were almost complete, Trump said: “We’re going to hit them extremely hard over the next two to three weeks, we’re going to bring them back to the stone ages.” (This is a threat of war crimes, to be clear.)

“Regime change was not our goal,” Trump added – a complete lie – before adding that the new leaders in Iran negotiating with the US are “less radical and much more reasonable.”

He quickly threatened that, if he and Iran can’t reach a deal, the US will carry out even more war crimes: “If there’s no deal, we are going to hit each and every one of their electric generating plants very hard and probably simultaneously.” He said the US could “hit their oil,” too.

“The whole world is watching,” Trump said, adding that “they can’t believe what they’re seeing – the brilliance of the United States military.”

When it came to paying tribute to members of the military who have died in his war, Trump muffed his lines. “As we celebrate this progress,” he said, “we think especially of the 13 American warriors who have laid down their lives in this fight to prevent our children from ever having to face a nuclear Iran. Twice this past month, I’ve traveled to Dover Air Force Base, and it’s been something.”

It’s been “something,” alright.

“We are on the cusp of ending Iran’s sinister threat to America and the world,” Trump concluded.

There is indeed a blundering, omnipresent, and sinister threat to the globe. Sadly for Trump, an increasing percentage of the world community and our own allies now identify that threat as a wholly American one.

🇮🇷 Iran War Updates

Death tolls:

Iran: Iran’s deputy health minister told Al Jazeera that at least 1,937 people had been killed in US-Israeli attacks. Iranian officials said earlier this month that nearly 20,000 people had been injured.

Lebanon: Israel has killed more than 1,315 people, including at least 125 children, in Lebanon, according to yesterday’s figures from the country’s Health Ministry.

US troops: At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.

Israel: At least 29 people, including 10 Israeli soldiers, have been killed and thousands injured in Iranian and Hezbollah attacks.

Elsewhere in the region: Dozens of others have been killed across the region, including four Palestinian women in the occupied West Bank.

items.title: Tapper vs. Piker: Is the CNN Anchor Auditioning for David Ellison?
items.author: Zeteo
items.content:
Good evening, and welcome to this week’s ‘Ragebait.’

Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex and Bari World. In tonight’s edition, we dig into how Jake Tapper taking on Hasan Piker is making many wonder if the CNN anchor is pandering to his soon-to-be new bosses – or maybe he’s just trying to make his dad happy. Plus, Donald Trump thinks a Bush-era neocon is “Brilliant” and CBS News is doing all white.

Tapper ‘Auditioning to be CNN’s Dokoupil’

Three weeks after drawing the ire of progressives for targeting Zohran Mamdani and his wife for hosting pro-Palestinian activist Mahmoud Khalil, CNN star Jake Tapper is once again under fire for taking aim at a left-wing critic of Israel and painting that person as antisemitic.

In this instance, Tapper’s target is popular progressive streamer Hasan Piker, who has suddenly found himself the focus of a cancellation campaign by centrist groups and right-wing Jewish publications looking to marginalize his influence in upcoming Democratic primaries.

Tapper has struggled with balancing his reporting on Israel and Palestine throughout the years. According to one former CNN colleague, the anchor – who is Jewish – would get calls from his father accusing him of “selling out” if he did anything too “Palestinian-friendly,” prompting Tapper to “do something that was kind of pro-Israel” shortly after.

This latest segment by Tapper, which featured a panel consisting of ‘Being Jewish’ podcaster Jonah Platt and no defenders of Piker, was roundly criticized by leftist journalists and commentators – including Mehdi himself – as “deeply irresponsible” for portraying Piker as a “fringe” figure peddling “Jew-hatred.”

The Monday afternoon CNN discussion, which came mere hours after Platt loudly defended Israel’s one-sided death penalty law aimed at only Palestinians, prompted accusations that Tapper was pandering to his soon-to-be corporate bosses – vocal Israel supporters and donors who recently hired a self-avowed “Zionist fanatic” to run CBS News.

“Tapper auditioning for the Ellisons to be CNN’s [Tony] Dokoupil,” ‘Majority Report’ co-host Emma Vigeland quipped on X, referencing the father-son owners of Paramount who just won their hostile takeover bid for Warner Bros. Discovery, the parent company of CNN.

“Tapper’s already auditioning for the Ellison takeover like he won’t be on Substack in 2 years, after being replaced by an AI Anchor who’s directly programmed by Smotrich,” comedian Francesca Fiorentini tweeted.

Of course, since the Ellisons purchased Paramount last year, CBS News has taken on a more conservative, pro-Israel stance under editor-in-chief Bari Weiss, the founder of the anti-woke, center-right blog The Free Press.

Piker’s “rise as a prominent voice on the left is now driving a wedge between Democrats – and for good reason,” Tapper declared during the Monday segment. “Hasan Piker’s past and present is checkered with controversial, if not outrageous, if not bigoted statements.”

Besides airing a handful of selectively edited clips of Piker’s comments, including one where he referred to Orthodox Jews in Israel as “inbred,” Tapper also read from a recent op-ed by centrist political group Third Way that argued Piker was “anti-American, anti-women, anti-Western and anti-Semitic. No Democrat should engage with him.”

“He’s able to sort of get away with some of this stuff in these subtle ways, when really his impact is just as harmful as any out-and-out antisemite,” Platt, who recently raged that Ms. Rachel “has a problem with Jews,” grumbled, asserting that Piker will “just sub ‘Jew’ for ‘Israel’” to provide cover for his supposed antisemitism.

This came a few weeks after Tapper brought on Liberal Fascism author and hardcore neocon Jonah Goldberg to rage about Mamdani and his wife, Rama Duwaji. Besides ludicrously likening Khalil to the Ku Klux Klan, Goldberg joined Tapper in criticizing Duwaji’s Instagram likes – which Weiss’s Free Press had been obsessively covering, fuming that the NYC first lady approved of “radical anti-Israel content.”

Shortly after the segment about Piker aired, the streamer reacted by saying it was “absolutely insane” that Tapper was doing a segment on his “antisemitism” on the same day that Israel signed a death penalty law for Palestinian detainees.

Others, meanwhile, pointed out that Piker’s “inbred” comments were largely taken out of context while taking Tapper to task for not having a pro-Palestinian voice on the panel.

Interestingly, just hours after Tapper’s segment calling on Piker to essentially be de-platformed, the left-wing Twitch streamer appeared on CNN anchor Elex Michaelson’s late-night broadcast to talk about his recent relief trip to Cuba. (And, like clockwork, the right-wing StopAntisemitism organization blasted CNN for giving Piker a “platform.”)

And while Tapper did not discuss the Israeli death penalty law during his Monday broadcast, he did air a report from correspondent Jeremy Diamond during Tuesday afternoon’s program. That also came two days after Tapper, in response to a separate story by Diamond, denounced the Israeli military’s detention and assault of a CNN crew reporting in the West Bank as “completely unacceptable.”

Though critics are currently joking that Tapper is currying favor with the Ellisons with these recent segments, it isn’t as if the CNN star has only recently taken aim at prominent Muslims or expressed pro-Israeli sentiments. In 2024, for instance, Tapper smeared Rep. Rashida Tlaib by putting words in her mouth, only to later say he merely “misspoke.”

One former longtime colleague of Tapper’s pointed out that the anchor has found himself under outside pressure in the past when reporting on Israel and Palestine.

“Everything is a performance,” the former colleague continued. “It’s all theater, and he is so practiced at that, so expert at that. He’s so careful about his reputation and how he’s perceived. It’s all just one hugely studied kind of performance.”

The ex-CNN staffer further observed that while “being very pro-Israel” was largely an uncontroversial stance to take for Tapper 10 years ago, the shift in public opinion amid the Gaza genocide – which has only been exacerbated recently by the Iran war – could put the CNN anchor in a precarious position.

“There is far more acceptance in polite society to talk about this stuff, especially after the protests on campus and ‘Free Palestine,’” the former colleague said. “And I’m curious how he manages that given that he wants to be on the popular side and be the hero.”

Representatives for CNN and Tapper declined to comment.

Trump Now Loves the Bush Neocons

Remember when Donald Trump was railing against the neoconservatives who “lied” us into the Iraq War, saying George W. Bush made a “big, fat mistake” while campaigning on ending the forever wars?

Now, it seems, all is forgiven. And it’s not just Mark Levin and Sean Hannity who are influencing Trump’s Iran decision-making through his TV screen.

Meet Marc Thiessen, a “Brilliant” member of Trump’s ‘Fox News Cabinet,’ who has become a presidential favorite in recent months as the commander-in-chief’s bloodlust for war has grown – further showing Trump’s embrace of the Bush-era neocons that he promised to eliminate from the GOP a decade ago.

See, before Thiessen was a Fox talking head, he served as the chief speechwriter in the George W. Bush White House. In other words, he was one of the principal salesmen for the disastrous Iraq quagmire – a war that Trump (falsely) claimed he always opposed.

It was his criticism of the Iraq War that boosted Trump’s 2016 electoral hopes, as Trump repeatedly hammered one-time frontrunner Jeb Bush during the GOP primary over his brother’s Middle East campaign, calling it the “worst decision” in presidential history.

Additionally, Thiessen had been a fairly vocal critic of Trump’s over the years, and even when it was clear that Trump was going to be the Republican nominee again in 2024, the ex-Bush flack groused about the “mistakes” Trump had made and how difficult it was to support his campaign.

However, with Trump following Bush’s lead in warmongering Middle East adventures, Thiessen has become a full-fledged MAGA cheerleader while looking to advise the president via Fox. And since Trump loves anyone who kisses his ass publicly, he has made sure to sing Thiessen’s praises.

In other words, as Vox’s Benjy Sarlin aptly pointed out last month, the president’s boosterism of a “famously hawkish” Bush alum who sold America on the Iraq War shows that Trump’s “‘neocon’ purge was made up.”

CBS News Keeps Getting More Right and White

In her continued effort to reshape CBS News into a larger version of The Free Press, Bari Weiss recently announced the expansion of the network’s investigative unit – featuring the hiring of one of her Free Press reporters, Gabe Kaminsky.

According to multiple network staffers that I spoke with, these latest moves – which Weiss has framed as part of her “huge emphasis on scoops” – are just further proof of the anti-DEI chief’s push to make CBS News whiter and more conservative.

“There does seem to be a deliberate purging of POC, women, and 50+ under both Weiss and [CBS News president Tom] Cibrowski, so it doesn’t surprise me the makeup of the new team,” a senior CBS News staffer said.

Along with Kaminsky, who came up through the right-wing organization Young America’s Foundation’s journalism program and spent his entire early career at conservative outlets, the CBS News investigative unit hired former Washington Post reporter Daniel Gilbert and brought in three other CBS journalists. All five of the new team members are white, essentially doubling the size of the unit – which was already overwhelmingly white.

With David Ellison having already promised the Trump administration he’d eliminate all DEI initiatives at Paramount as part of his purchase of the company, recent layoffs at CBS News have seemed to disproportionately impact minorities and women. For instance, during last fall’s crippling cuts, all eight on-air personalities let go were female, and half were people of color. The network also eliminated its Race and Culture Unit, and a Black producer who was let go said CBS was engaging in “race-based layoffs.”

Meanwhile, most of the new full-time staffers that Weiss has recently brought in – such as Israeli-flag-waving correspondent Jonah Kaplan – have one thing in common.

“I may be wrong, but I think that since Bari started – she hasn’t hired a single person of color except for that racism denier Inaya Iman,” one CBS reporter told me, with the senior staffer adding: “There’s one POC woman [Weiss] hired in London. Inaya Folarin Iman. Look her up, read her stuff, and you’ll see why.”

Indeed, two weeks ago, Iman – a Black Lives Matter critic and a former Brexit party candidate who hosted a show on right-wing British network GB News – announced that she was joining CBS as a reporter.

In response to this story, a CBS News spokesperson sent the following Trumpian response: “Please refer to our previous statement about how Zeteo’s ‘coverage’ of CBS News is all fabricated lies. No exception here.”

Watch out for my newsletter, ‘Ragebait,’ every week. I focus on the right-wing outrage-industrial complex and the intersection of our political and media landscapes. Send any tips on Signal (justinbaragona.85).
